title: Conversation entity schema designed; second per-entity governance schema specification produced
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `conversation` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/conversation.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight orientation. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo and read the eight-item reading list named in the kickoff: crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, governance-schema-specs/workstream.md (the just-completed predecessor specification), the six foundation decisions DEC-117..122 plus the six SES-048 decisions DEC-123..128 from db-export/decisions.json, SES-046, SES-047, and SES-048 from db-export/sessions.json, crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py, and methodology-schema-specs/domain.md as the structural exemplar. API health check (against the conversation's sandbox localhost, not Doug's machine) and pytest run (sparse checkout doesn't include tests/) were flagged as not meaningfully runnable in the conversation environment per the SES-048 precedent; design-only conversation, so neither gates.

2. Cross-spec precedents inherited from SES-048 surfaced at orientation: references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships, per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped lifecycles, and terminal-states-are-terminal discipline. Items already pre-committed by workstream.md and not re-decided by this conversation: the kind `conversation_belongs_to_workstream` (its name and direction were settled in workstream.md; this conversation registers it in vocab.py but does not re-decide it); ENTITY_TYPES additions `conversation` and `reference_book` (already listed in workstream.md's additions table).

3. Drift surfaced before opening Decision 1. The kickoff's working assumption for the conversation-to-session linkage was 'add a conversation_id foreign key to session in the build (back-fillable for historical records), keep direct relationship simple.' But workstream.md locked references-edge as the cross-spec precedent for parent-child governance relationships, and the conversation-to-session relationship is structurally parent-child (a conversation produces zero or one sessions per DEC-013). The drift is exactly analogous to the SES-048 drift on the workstream-to-conversation relationship; the locked precedent binds unless clear cause to deviate. The kickoff's explicit 'do NOT modify sessions table shape' boundary reinforces the precedent — the FK alternative would modify the append-only sessions table per DEC-013, the references-edge alternative does not. No other drifts.

4. Decision 1 — conversation-to-session relationship mechanism. Eight-element template. Resolved in favour of references-edge per the SES-048 precedent. New relationship kind named: conversation_records_session (source=conversation, target=session, source-first per DEC-048, past-tense 'records' mirrors workstream_planned_in_reference_book's past-participle posture). Cardinality at-most-one outgoing per conversation per DEC-013. Required-when rule: edge required when conversation_status='complete', enforced at the access layer with the same combined-validation pattern as workstream's supersession-requires-edge rule (HTTP 422 with {error: complete_conversation_requires_session_edge}). Approved Option A (references-edge).

5. Decision 2 — lifecycle granularity. Eight-element template. Resolved to keep all seven states from DEC-119 in full (planned → kickoff_drafted → ready → in_flight → one of complete / cancelled / superseded). The kickoff_drafted-vs-ready distinction is operationally real and was demonstrated by the project's own conversation lineage at the kickoff's authoring time: workstream's kickoff was drafted and ready, conversation's kickoff was drafted but not ready (waiting on workstream to close), reference_book's kickoff was drafted but two conversations away from openable. Collapsing the two states would lose the operationally meaningful distinction. Inherited truly-terminal posture from workstream's precedent (no transitions out of complete/cancelled/superseded, including between terminal states). Added the complete-requires-session-edge rule per Decision 1; supersession-requires-edge rule inherited verbatim from workstream. Forward-only planning lifecycle (no regression within the four non-terminal states either). Six per-status lifecycle timestamps named (one per non-starter status; planned_at collapses into created_at per workstream precedent): conversation_kickoff_drafted_at, conversation_ready_at, conversation_started_at, conversation_completed_at, conversation_cancelled_at, conversation_superseded_at. Approved Option A (full seven states).

6. Decision 3 — conversation-to-kickoff (work_ticket) relationship. Eight-element template. Resolved to references-edge with tentative new kind conversation_opens_against_work_ticket (active verb, present tense, source=conversation). Cardinality at-most-one outgoing per conversation. Required-when rule: edge required when conversation_status is in {kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, complete}; optional for {planned, cancelled, superseded}. Kind-name posture: committed tentatively here parallel to workstream.md's tentative workstream_planned_in_reference_book commitment; the work_ticket schema-design conversation may refine for verb-tense consistency with work_ticket's other inbound kinds; build-planning reconciles. ENTITY_TYPES gains work_ticket. Approved Option A.

7. Decision 4 — predecessor/successor chaining between conversations. Eight-element template. Resolved to introduce new kind conversation_succeeds_conversation (source=successor, target=predecessor, source-first per DEC-048). Diverges from workstream.md's defer-sequencing posture on the strength of conversation-sequencing frequency: every conversation in a sequenced workstream has predecessors and successors, captured today only in in_flight_at_end fields of session records (the weak-coupling pattern DEC-121 says to eliminate), while workstream sequencing is comparatively rare. Cardinality many-to-many (a build-planning conversation has six predecessor schema-design conversations; a workstream-establishing conversation has multiple direct successors). No required-when rule. Cycle prevention deferred to build-planning. New cross-spec precedent established: typed sequencing edges are introduced when entity-family frequency justifies, distinct from workstream's defer-sequencing posture. The remaining four schemas inherit the precedent and may deviate with rationale. Approved Option A.

8. After Decision 4 Doug instructed Claude to 'Proceed with making your recommendations final decision and complete the prompt without asking for further input.' Routine items 5-15 from the proposed sequence were resolved as a single batch and folded directly into the spec draft.

9. Routine items resolved without per-decision confirmation: (a) identifier prefix CONV, four letters matching PROC/CRMC precedent; (b) field inventory minimum-viable — no estimated_duration, no priority, no conversation_kind, intended-deliverable description folded into conversation_description; (c) workstream membership mandatory at the access layer, enforced as 'exactly one outgoing conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge'; (d) per-status lifecycle timestamps as enumerated in Decision 2; (e) cross-workstream conversation references use existing generic references/is_about kinds, no new vocabulary; (f) soft-delete with restore matching workstream's posture, distinct from cancelled status; (g) standard 8-endpoint API surface; (h) UI deviations — master pane gains a Workstream column as the fifth column (vs workstream's four), plus workstream_identifier and status filter combos in the panel toolbar realising the new query-string parameters on the list endpoint; (i) eighteen acceptance criteria (parallel to workstream's sixteen, with two added for the workstream-membership-required rule and the new list-endpoint filters); (j) vocabulary additions named in the spec table for build-planning aggregation; (k) cross-references section mirrors workstream.md's structure with the additional six SES-048 decisions cited as related prior decisions.

10. Spec drafting in one pass per spec guide section 4.3. governance-schema-specs/conversation.md drafted following the structural shape of governance-schema-specs/workstream.md, with all nine required subsections of section 3 substantively filled. Eighteen testable acceptance criteria captured (spec guide gate requires 10). Section 3.8 categorized by build / backfill / future tags per the spec guide convention with five build items (sidebar grouping, migration ordering, tentative kind names on both outbound and inbound sides, backfill behavior on create with terminal status, cycle prevention in conversation_succeeds_conversation), five backfill items inheriting from PI-022 plus new items specific to this entity (historical lifecycle timestamps, status assignment for prior conversations, predecessor-successor edges from in_flight_at_end text, kickoff-to-work_ticket edges from seed prompts, mandatory-workstream-membership for ad-hoc conversations like SES-046), and five future items (estimated duration, priority, conversation kind/type, outcome summary, pause/resume states, cross-workstream typed kinds). Committed at 6580a32 in one commit.

11. Close-out artifacts authored. After spec commit, this payload (ses_049.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-049.md) were drafted in one pass. Identifier check at close-out: latest applied SES is SES-048 so SES-049 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-128 so DEC-129..134 is next-available for the six decisions; no new planning items (PI-022 covers retroactive backfill including this conversation's records).

conversation_reference: Claude.ai audit-and-finalize conversation for the reference_book entity schema — third per-entity schema-design conversation in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Opened against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-reference-book.md. Discovered on the first pre-flight check that the kickoff's deliverable (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md, 467 lines) had already been drafted and committed by a previous Claude.ai sandbox session at c1d007e on 05-21-26 14:55, but that prior conversation never authored a session record or a close-out payload. The state mismatch between the local sandbox clone (one commit ahead of origin) and the V2 database (no SES-050 or DEC-135..140 yet recorded) surfaced the gap. The conversation transitioned from schema-design mode to audit-and-finalize mode: a structured audit pass against the schema spec guide §7.1 completeness gate and §6 cross-spec consistency requirements, against the workstream master plan's position-3-of-6 constraints, against DEC-117's family-1 classification, and against the predecessor specs' (workstream.md, conversation.md) declarations the spec relies on. The audit concluded the spec is substantively complete, methodologically disciplined, and passes every completeness and consistency criterion — with one factual error appearing in three places (the Change Log v1.0 paragraph, the DEC-138 description in §3.9.1, and the DEC-117 entry in §3.9.3) that misidentified the three `reference_book_kind` enum values that are additions beyond DEC-117's seven. Doug accepted the audit and authorized executing the Path 1 correction-and-close-out plan: fix the three text errors (substituting `architecture_document`, `conduct_framework`, `investigation_report` for the wrongly-named `workstream_master_plan`, `apply_script`, `session_startup_document` — those three are explicitly in DEC-117's seven per the DEC-117 decision record), bump the spec to v1.1 with Revision Control and Change Log entries documenting the correction, then author the close-out payload and apply prompt for SES-050. The conversation also surfaced a class-level prevention question: the state mismatch happened because the prior sandbox session committed the spec but never ran the close-out apply, leaving git and the V2 database silently out of sync. Doug asked for a prevention recommendation; the response named a workstream-state reconciliation utility (`tools/workstream_reconcile.py`) invoked from every kickoff prompt's pre-flight that compares the workstream's git state against the V2 database state and surfaces any committed-but-not-applied deliverables before architectural discussion begins. No planning item authored for the reconciliation utility in this payload; that question is flagged at the end of the close-out and Doug decides whether to add it. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
in_flight_at_end:

title: Reference book entity schema designed; third per-entity governance schema specification produced (audit-and-finalize)
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `reference_book` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight orientation. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo state at session open. Confirmed `crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md` is the session-startup entry per Doug's confirmation. On the first `git log` check, found that the local sandbox clone is one commit ahead of origin/main: c1d007e "v2: reference book schema spec — third of six governance entity schemas" dated 05-21-26 14:55 by author Doug, adding a complete 467-line `reference_book.md` spec. Cross-checked the V2 db-export: decisions through DEC-134, sessions through SES-049, no DEC-135..140 or SES-050 yet. Conclusion: a prior Claude.ai sandbox session drafted and committed the spec but never authored a session record or close-out payload; the V2 database is silently out of sync with the local git state. Surfaced this immediately to Doug.

2. Prevention discussion. Doug asked how to prevent the class of issue (sandbox commits spec → never closes out → V2 app shows stale workstream state → next conversation starts from stale assumption). Response framed the root cause as a two-system-of-record gap (git holds committed deliverables; V2 database holds governance state; the bridge — manual close-out apply — is a separate step that can be skipped). Recommended: a workstream-state reconciliation check at every kickoff pre-flight. Concretely a small `tools/workstream_reconcile.py` that takes a workstream identifier, asks the V2 API for that workstream's conversations and their statuses, asks git for commits touching the workstream's deliverable directory, and cross-references — any commit whose deliverable is not referenced by some session record's artifacts_produced is a stale-state signal. Alternatives considered (automatic close-out from the sandbox, V2 desktop app `last_processed_commit_hash` warnings, co-commit hooks) and rejected as either infeasible (sandbox can't reach Doug's local API) or higher-effort with smaller coverage. Flagged at conversation close for Doug's planning-item decision — not added to this payload.

3. Path selection. Three paths presented: (1) adopt the existing c1d007e commit and close out; (2) discard the commit and redo schema design from scratch; (3) audit-first then choose. Recommended Path 3. Doug accepted Path 3.

4. Audit execution. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, governance-schema-specs/workstream.md (predecessor 1), governance-schema-specs/conversation.md (predecessor 2), DEC-117 from db-export/decisions.json, sessions SES-048 and SES-049 from db-export/sessions.json, plus the spec file itself in full. Audited against four dimensions: schema-spec methodology guide §7.1 per-spec completeness gate (all nine §3 subsections present and substantively filled, §3.7 ≥10 testable criteria, §3.8 explicit category tags, §3.9 cites required references); §6 cross-spec consistency requirements (identifier prefix style, format, status field naming, status values, default starter, relationship-kind naming, field naming, timestamp inheritance with explicit deviation documented, soft-delete, identifier auto-assignment, API envelope); workstream master plan position-3-of-6 and constraints (RB-NNN working assumption realized, version history and 'in force at' semantics realized); predecessor-spec consistency (workstream.md line 123 declares `workstream_planned_in_reference_book`, verified; conversation.md generic-kinds posture for conversation→reference_book, verified).

5. Audit findings. Conclusion: the spec passes the per-spec completeness gate on every criterion and conforms to the §6 cross-spec consistency requirements with one explicit, justified deviation (base timestamps only for documentary-shaped lifecycles, established as a new cross-spec precedent in §1 and DEC-137). The spec also establishes a well-motivated parent-plus-child versioning model that diverges from charter/status's singleton-with-payload pattern with rationale (file content lives in git/files, database tracks metadata only). One substantive factual error identified: the Change Log v1.0 paragraph at line 21, the DEC-138 description at line 420, and the DEC-117 entry in §3.9.3 at line 438 each named the three `reference_book_kind` enum values that are additions beyond DEC-117 as `workstream_master_plan`, `apply_script`, `session_startup_document`. Per the DEC-117 decision record's verbatim text, those three values are explicitly listed in DEC-117's seven artifacts. The actual three additions beyond DEC-117 — present in the eleven-value enum but absent from DEC-117's seven — are `architecture_document`, `conduct_framework`, `investigation_report`. Doug accepted the audit and authorized Path 1 (correct-and-close-out).

6. Correction execution. Five str_replace operations on `governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md`: (a) update header `Last Updated` to 05-21-26 15:30; (b) bump header `Status` to `Draft v1.1` with annotation; (c) append a v1.1 row to the Revision Control table documenting the correction; (d) prepend a v1.1 paragraph to the Change Log section documenting the correction and update the v1.0 paragraph to be factually correct (architecture_document, conduct_framework, investigation_report); (e) correct DEC-138 description in §3.9.1; (f) correct DEC-117 entry in §3.9.3. All five operations verified — the enum declaration at line 96 (originally line 93 pre-edit) was correct in v1.0 and is unchanged. The two surviving mentions of the wrongly-named triple are now in the v1.1 Revision Control row and v1.1 Change Log paragraph, where they document the correction itself, which is the correct location.

7. Close-out payload and apply prompt authored. This payload (ses_050.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-050.md) drafted in one pass. Identifier check: latest applied SES is SES-049, so SES-050 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-134, so DEC-135..140 is next-available for the six decisions; no new planning items in this payload (PI-022 covers retroactive backfill including this conversation's records implicitly; the reconciliation-utility planning item is flagged at close-out for Doug's decision but not added).

8. Decisions authored (DEC-135..140 — see the decisions array below):
   - DEC-135: `reference_book` identifier prefix and format — RB-NNN, two-letter form acceptable per DEC-123, kickoff's open-question concern about visual ambiguity resolved by checking remaining-three-conversations' working prefixes (WT, COP, DEP) for confirmed non-collision.
   - DEC-136: `reference_book` versioning model — parent record plus child versions table. Distinct from charter/status's singleton-with-payload pattern because the file content lives in git/files and the database tracks version metadata only. Denormalized current-version pointer fields on the parent stay in sync with child rows via access-layer recompute on any child-table write. Establishes the documentary-entity versioning pattern for the project.
   - DEC-137: `reference_book` documentary-shaped lifecycle with three statuses and base timestamps only. active → archived / superseded transition map with the truly-terminal posture inherited verbatim from workstream and the supersession-requires-edge access-layer rule mirroring workstream's pattern. Establishes new cross-spec precedent: documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only, deviating from DEC-126's per-status-timestamps-for-workflow-shapes precedent. The remaining three governance specs apply the documentary-vs-workflow distinction on their own facts.
   - DEC-138: `reference_book_kind` closed enum with eleven values — covering all seven of DEC-117's named artifact types (product_requirements_document, implementation_plan, workstream_master_plan, methodology_guide, schema_specification, apply_script, session_startup_document) plus three additional types observed in the project's operating history beyond DEC-117 (architecture_document, conduct_framework, investigation_report) plus an `other` sentinel. The seven-versus-three split is documented honestly per the v1.1 correction.
   - DEC-139: `reference_book` field inventory plus file-path semantics plus engagement-scoping plus soft-delete posture. Seven content/classification fields plus three file-pointer fields plus three inherited base timestamps (no per-status timestamps per DEC-137). Repo-relative file path with no leading slash and no `..` segments; resolved at use time against the consuming repository. No engagement-scoping flag (per V2's per-engagement isolation, scoping is implicit). Default soft-delete with restore, distinct from `archived` and `superseded` status outcomes.
   - DEC-140: `reference_book` API surface plus version-management sub-endpoints plus master-pane Kind column plus default UI with inline version-history plus sixteen acceptance criteria. Standard 8-endpoint set for the parent plus three version-management sub-endpoints (GET /reference-books/{id}/versions, POST /reference-books/{id}/versions, GET /reference-books/{id}/version-at?as_of=...) realizing the in-force-at-time-T semantics named in the kickoff. List-endpoint filters `kind` and `status` alongside `include_deleted`. Master-pane Kind column as third column. Inline version-history section in the detail pane with Add Version affordance. Sixteen acceptance criteria captured.

artifacts_produced:
Three files committed to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ in the crmbuilder repo during this conversation:

(1) governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md (471 lines, v1.0). Fourth per-entity schema specification in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Conforms to governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Establishes the work_ticket entity type for V2 storage with six content/classification fields (identifier, title, description, notes, kind, status), one file-pointer field (file_path), and seven timestamp columns (three inherited base plus four per-status lifecycle: work_ticket_ready_at, work_ticket_consumed_at, work_ticket_cancelled_at, work_ticket_superseded_at). Five-status workflow-shaped lifecycle (drafted → ready → consumed plus terminals cancelled and superseded) with truly-terminal terminals, the supersession-requires-edge rule inherited from DEC-125, and a new consumed-requires-edge rule establishing the inverse-direction terminal-edge-requirement precedent. Single-use enforcement rule (at most one inbound consumption edge per work_ticket, regardless of status) as the schema-level realization of DEC-117's family-2 definition. Four-value closed kind enum (kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other) with the sentinel as documented escape hatch. Standard 8-endpoint API set with `?kind=` and `?status=` filter parameters. Default UI layout with master-pane Kind column (third column position) and Status filter combo in the toolbar. Default soft-delete with restore. Sixteen acceptance criteria. No new relationship-kind vocabulary required — the entity type itself was already named by conversation.md §3.3.4 and the inbound consumption edge by conversation.md's `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` registration. Pre-existed as an untracked sandbox file at session open; adopted without modification after audit.

(2) close-out-payloads/ses_051.json (new — this payload). One session (SES-051), six decisions (DEC-141..146), zero planning items, seven references (six decided_in plus one is_about SES-051 → PI-022).

(3) prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-051.md (new — this payload's apply prompt). Idempotent apply targeting the CRMBUILDER engagement.

Via this payload's apply prompt, the following records land in the CRMBUILDER engagement database:

(4) SES-051 — this session record.
(5) DEC-141 — WT identifier prefix and format.
(6) DEC-142 — boundary discipline with reference_book (intent-at-creation, no re-categorization, file_path coexistence).
(7) DEC-143 — workflow-shaped lifecycle, five statuses, truly-terminal terminals, consumed-requires-edge rule (new cross-spec precedent), supersession-requires-edge rule.
(8) DEC-144 — single-use enforcement (at most one inbound consumption edge).
(9) DEC-145 — field inventory with four per-status lifecycle timestamps; declines tentative `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` kind; declines typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind.
(10) DEC-146 — API surface; UI defaults with Kind column and Status filter combo; soft-delete posture; closed four-value kind enum; sixteen acceptance criteria.
(11) Six decided_in references (DEC-141..146 → SES-051) plus one is_about reference (SES-051 → PI-022) mirroring SES-048/049/050's pattern of linking each schema-design conversation to the retroactive-backfill planning item.

No source code modified — this is a schema-design conversation producing one specification only. Vocabulary changes, schema migrations, and access-layer methods are aggregated by the build-planning conversation after all six schemas exist.

conversation_reference: Claude.ai ARCHITECTURE-mode conversation that opened against the open question of how to express deployment-time security declaratively in CRM Builder's YAML schema. The conversation walked the design space in two consequential decisions: first, the fundamental fork between definitions-only and definitions-plus-assignments scope for the YAML (resolved as Option A, definitions only); second, the reordering of Category 6 of the gap analysis between schema v1.2 and v1.3 (resolved as Option C, scope-level + teams in v1.2, field-level deferred to v1.3). Between decisions the conversation produced a strawman security block grounded in a Mentor role for Cleveland Business Mentors, then ran a code-verification pass against the actual loader and validator in espo_impl/core/config_loader.py to confirm the existing top-level key set and the absence of any security/roles/teams recognition. After the second decision locked, the gap-analysis Section 6 was rewritten end-to-end to express the new five-part v1.2 scope (Parts A–E) and the deferred v1.3 scope (old Parts B and C), with Q1–Q3 amended and Q7–Q8 added. A new UPDATE-PROMPT was then authored as the kickoff for the next conversation (drafting the operator-facing reference sections of app-yaml-schema.md for v1.2 Category 6 Parts A–E). title: YAML schema Category 6 (Role-Based Access Control) reordered across v1.2 and v1.3; gap-analysis Section 6 amended and v1.2 drafting kickoff committed
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Lets discuss deploying security with YAML".

Landscape framing. Opened by mapping the EspoCRM security model into its constituent layers (roles, teams, scope-level access, field-level access, assignment permission, portal roles, user-to-role and user-to-team assignments) and naming the one fork that needed to settle before any schema work could begin: whether the YAML owns role and team *definitions* only or *definitions + assignments*. Side-flagged the existing memory note that YAML schema Category 6 was deferred to v1.2, with verification of current status to come once a direction was chosen.

Decision 1 — definitions-only security model (Option A). Doug chose Option A: YAML defines roles and teams; user-to-role and user-to-team assignments are runtime data managed in the target CRM UI. Locked as DEC-153. Rationale: matches the prevailing IAM industry pattern (policy as code, identity membership as runtime state); avoids requiring a YAML edit and Configure run for every new user. The CBM scenario of a new mentor onboarded mid-cycle was the concrete motivator.

Portal and field-level scope confirmation. Confirmed no portal in CBM's plans, so Portal Roles drop from v1.0 scope. Confirmed (by default, not reopened) that the original Category 6 field-level deferral to v1.2 held at this point in the conversation; this would be revisited at Decision 2.

Strawman drafting. Produced a strawman security block grounded in a CBM Mentor role: top-level `security:` containing `teams:` (Mentors, Administrators, Fundraising) and `roles:` with one fully-realized Mentor role showing `scope_access:` per entity (c-Engagement, c-Session, Contact, Account) using the EspoCRM grain (create as yes/no; read/edit/delete/stream as all/team/own/no), plus `system_permissions:` covering assignment_permission, user_permission, export, mass_update, audit_log. Flagged three implementation concerns: YAML boolean coercion on bare `no`; the limit of EspoCRM's role scope levels for expressing linked-record access (Mentor → Contact via Engagement); naming convention for built-in vs. custom entities. Doug approved the structure and noted values can be revised against the final security design.

Code verification — does the YAML processor handle any of this today? Conversation pivoted from PRD/schema work to code investigation per the working pattern that mandates verifying behavior against the actual codebase. Doug confirmed crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md as the reference. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo with the project's standard sparse pattern; read CLAUDE.md head; inspected espo_impl/core/config_loader.py for top-level key recognition; inspected the validate_program() function for unknown-key enforcement; searched for any role or team manager files in espo_impl/core/ and confirmed none exist; located the existing Category 6 plan in PRDs/product/yaml-schema-gap-analysis-MR-pilot.md and read the four-part structure (Parts A–D, with Part D added 05-03-26 covering panel and layout role-aware visibility).

Gap-analysis findings reported to Doug. (1) Loader recognizes exactly five top-level keys: version, description, content_version, entities, relationships. No security:, roles:, or teams: handling. (2) validate_program() applies no top-level key whitelist; an unknown top-level key is silently ignored (no error, no warning, no effect). (3) No role manager, team manager, or API client wrappers for /api/v1/Role or /api/v1/Team exist. Greenfield. (4) EspoCRM's REST API exposes Role and Team as first-class CRUD entities — not in the no-public-write-path list (which contains only saved views, duplicate checks, workflows). (5) The existing v1.2 plan in the gap analysis is field-level-centric; it covers Part A (roles declaration), Part B (field-level permissions), Part C (permission presets), and Part D (panel/layout role-aware visibility). Critically, it covers no teams concept at all and no scope-level entity access (CRUD per role at all/team/own/no). The strawman from earlier in the conversation tackled exactly what was missing from the existing plan. The two scopes are complementary, not competing.

Decision 2 — Category 6 reordering (Option C). Surfaced the gap between the existing v1.2 plan (field-level + presets) and the strawman scope (scope-level + teams + system permissions) as a consequential decision passing the two-part test. Three options presented: (A) narrow v1.2 — ship the existing plan only, scope-level and teams remain manual config; (B) expanded v1.2 — ship both, double the surface area; (C) reorder — ship scope-level + teams as v1.2, field-level shifts to v1.3. Recommended C with one-line rationale that scope-level access is more universal (every role needs entity-level CRUD bounds; only some roles need field-level overrides) and that field-level was acknowledged in the original plan as needing additional pilot input. Doug chose C. Locked as DEC-154.

Gap-analysis Section 6 amendment. With Option C locked, the gap-analysis Section 6 was rewritten end-to-end in a single commit (eb1d7e7). New five-part v1.2 structure: Part A — Roles declaration (largely preserved from original, name-as-identity, persona metadata); Part B — Teams declaration (NEW); Part C — Scope-level entity access (NEW) with whitelist semantics, vocabulary documented, YAML boolean coercion called out; Part D — System permissions (NEW); Part E — Panel and layout role-aware visibility (was original Part D, renumbered, content preserved). New deferred-to-v1.3 section covers old Part B (field-level permissions) and old Part C (permission presets). Q1 amended (still defer to v1.2, with explicit part split); Q2 amended (file renamed from programs/roles.yaml to programs/security.yaml to reflect roles + teams); Q3 amended (presets deferred to v1.3); Q7 added (user-to-role and user-to-team assignments are out of YAML scope, capturing the DEC-153 outcome at the gap-analysis level); Q8 added (whitelist semantics for scope_access: omission denies). Change log entry recorded at the top of the document.

Next-step kickoff prompt. UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md authored at PRDs/product/ as the kickoff for the next Claude.ai conversation, which will draft the operator-facing reference sections of app-yaml-schema.md covering v1.2 Category 6 Parts A–E. The kickoff names three decisions likely to surface in that session (section placement, file-type concept, deploy-ordering expression) and constrains the session to documentation drafting only (no code, no Claude Code prompt authoring, no design extension beyond what gap-analysis Section 6 specifies). Initial commit at f152181.

Push of the two pending commits (gap analysis amendment, UPDATE-PROMPT) hit a non-fast-forward error; git pull --rebase incorporated remote changes (originating from a different recent conversation's commits), which rewrote the local commit SHAs. The kickoff prompt's `Created in:` line still referenced the pre-rebase SHA 3d883b9; corrected to eb1d7e7 in a follow-up commit at 4ff2e43.

Close-out artifact authoring. This payload (ses_053.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-053.md) authored at conversation close. The session record is bundled into the payload per the operative SES-046-through-SES-052 precedent. Identifier check at close-out time against the v2 database snapshot (db-export/sessions.json, decisions.json, planning_items.json): latest applied SES is SES-052; next-available is SES-053. Latest applied DEC is DEC-152; next-available is DEC-153 and DEC-154 for the two decisions. Latest applied PI is PI-023; no new planning item is created in this session (the next-conversation kickoff is committed as a work artifact, not deferred as an open question). title: deposit_event entity schema designed; governance-entity schema-design workstream concluded; build-planning kickoff drafted
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `deposit_event` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/deposit_event.md` — the complete schema specification per the template in `governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md`. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern. This conversation also produces one additional artifact: the kickoff prompt for the build-planning conversation that closes the workstream."

Structured summary of the architectural discussion:

The conversation drove six architectural decisions, one at a time, with full discussion at each step before the user's terse approval. The six decisions and their resolutions:

(1) DEC-155 — Identifier prefix and format. Adopted DEP-NNN zero-padded to three digits. Three-letter form on the strength of the dominant three-letter precedent (DEC, REF, COP). Pairs naturally with COP for the family-3 deposit-bucket reading. Alternatives DEPT, DEPOS, EVT, APPLY rejected for disambiguation, length, or semantic mismatch.

(2) DEC-156 — Lifecycle shape. The primary architectural decision of the conversation. The user pushed back on the initial recommendation (born-terminal append-only) by asking about continuous-status visibility for failure diagnosis. Discussion surfaced three real failure scenarios (clean failure handled equally by both options; unclean crash where workflow-staged would capture stuck-running state; long apply where continuous status would help). A third synthesis option emerged: born-terminal append-only with diagnostic log file capture via a deposit_event_log_file_path field. The user adopted the synthesis. Establishes a new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only with creation as the event-recording moment — a third lifecycle category alongside workflow-shaped and documentary.

(3) DEC-157 — Back-reference mechanism. Adopted references-table edges with one new generic-verb kind deposit_event_wrote_record spanning four target types (session, decision, planning_item, reference) in one vocabulary entry. Rejected per-target-type variants (A′), JSON column (B), dedicated back-reference table (C), target-side columns (D) on grounds of references-edge precedent, established generic-verb naming pattern, and minimum-viable schema growth. Settled cardinality, 409-SKIP semantics (skipped records not back-referenced), back-reference scope (substantive content layer only, no recursive back-references).

(4) DEC-158 — Re-apply semantics. Adopted multi-event-per-payload: a close_out_payload may carry zero or more inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edges, one per apply attempt. Establishes a second new cross-spec precedent: born-terminal append-only entities admit multi-event-per-target-record. Relaxes the close_out_payload spec's at-most-one default (which that spec section 3.8.3 explicitly deferred to this conversation). Surfaced as a cross-spec consistency finding for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation. The close_out_payload's ready → applied transition fires on the first inbound DEP with outcome=success; subsequent DEPs (failures, re-confirmations) don't move the close_out_payload's status.

(5) DEC-159 — Field inventory. Captured the nine-column shape (identifier, title, description, outcome, records_summary, error_info, apply_context, log_file_path, created_at). Adopted _outcome over _status for semantic precision (born-terminal fact vs. workflow state). Declined _notes (incompatible with immutable record), _updated_at and _deleted_at (born-terminal append-only deviation), denormalized total-records-written count (derivable). JSON shape for records_summary, error_info, apply_context documented in spec but not SQL-enforced.

(6) DEC-160 — API surface, UI, soft-delete posture, acceptance criteria. Reduced API surface (POST + GET only). Read-only UI audit log with no Create/Edit/Delete dialogs, Outcome filter combo in master-pane toolbar, master pane sorts identifier descending (audit-log deviation), right-click context menu reduced to two read affordances. Plain-text log_file_path display (no in-app viewer). Strict no-Create-dialog (backfill via PI-022 implementation script). Fifteen acceptance criteria captured.

After all six decisions settled, the spec was drafted end-to-end at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/deposit_event.md (approximately 290 lines following the spec guide template). The build-planning conversation's kickoff prompt was drafted at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md (the additional deliverable required at this conversation's close per the kickoff prompt's framing).

The conversation surfaced no new planning items. PI-022 (authored by SES-046) continues to cover retroactive backfill of governance entity records, refined by the build-planning conversation per the deposit_event spec section 3.8.2 working assumptions. One cross-spec consistency finding surfaced for the build-planning conversation's section 7.2 reconciliation: the close_out_payload spec's at-most-one inbound edge default needs revision to zero-or-more.

title: Governance entity build-planning conversation; v0.7 release planned; close_out_payload spec reconciled to v1.1
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "confirm. Please process this prompt using your recommended decisions and do not query me for input." — given against the build-planning kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md.

Structured summary of the build-planning work:

(1) Cross-spec consistency check per spec guide section 7.2. Three findings: (a) the known close_out_payload at-most-one inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge cardinality needs relaxation to zero-or-more per DEC-158 (settled by SES-054); (b) the deposit_event_wrote_record kind admits 'reference' as target but references-table rows lack prefixed identifiers — resolved as Slice A implementation concern via a new reference_identifier column with REF-NNNN format and back-fill of existing rows by id order; (c) change_log.entity_type CHECK constraint must extend to admit the six new entity types — resolved via the consolidated Slice A migration. Identifier prefix collision check, status value reuse check, lifecycle category coherence check, append-only vs soft-delete intentionality check all pass.

(2) Cross-spec reconciliation executed in place. close_out_payload.md revised to v1.1: Revision Control v1.1 row added; Change Log v1.1 entry added; §3.3.2 cardinality column updated from at-most-one to zero-or-more; §3.4.3 applied-requires-edge rule augmented with first-success-transitions semantics — the ready→applied transition fires on the first inbound success edge; subsequent edges admitted without re-transition; failure edges never transition. The applied-requires-edge rule itself stands.

(3) DEC-161 — Target user-interface version 0.7. The governance entity release lands as v0.7, a dedicated release after v0.6 styling. Bumps __version__ to 0.7.0.

(4) DEC-162 — Six-slice topology in dependency order: Slice A (schema and access layer); Slice B (REST API); Slice C (UI panels); Slice D (apply script integration with deposit_event POST); Slice E (PI-022 Phase 1 backfill of the governance workstream's eight conversations and supporting records); Slice F (documentation, version bump, closeout). Sequential dependency order with each slice gating on the prior slice's acceptance criteria.

title: Code Change Lifecycle workstream established: diagnosed planning_items resolution gap, adopted first-class commit entities (Option III), authored kickoff for methodology drafting conversation
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Why is there only one planning item with a status of resolved in the crmbuilder V2 database. We have completed a number of steps, so I would expect there to be completed records in the planning items database?"

Structured summary of the diagnostic-and-design work:

(1) Initial hypothesis framing. Proposed two likely causes for the under-resolution: a close-out discipline gap (the apply flow never transitions prior planning_items) or a vocabulary gap (no resolution-style relationship_kind exists). Authored a diagnostic script and committed it to tools/diagnostics/diagnose_planning_items.py (commit dde4eb4) after Doug picked option 1 (use existing tools/diagnostics/ directory) over option 2 (new automation/diagnostics/) on the placement question. Authored a Claude Code investigation prompt at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-diagnose-planning-items.md (commit 77010bf) for Doug to run.

(2) Diagnostic execution. Claude Code ran the prompt against ~/Dropbox/Projects/crmbuilder/crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db. Status distribution: 25 Open, 1 Resolved (PI-021), total 26. The one Resolved record is a same-workstream same-day create-and-resolve case: PI-021 created in SES-044 on 2026-05-19, status flipped to Resolved on 2026-05-20 with no companion edge and resolution_reference left NULL. The planning_items.resolution_reference column exists as VARCHAR(64) — clear past design intent — but is NULL on every row including PI-021. The refs vocabulary has 17 relationship_kinds and zero resolution-style kinds for planning_items; decisions get decided_in (163 rows, a true resolution edge), planning_items get only is_about (27 rows, overloaded) and blocks (2 rows). Three diagnostic script bugs identified: table is named refs not references; the PRAGMA call was unquoted; the status filter status != 'open' is case-sensitive against title-case values Open and Resolved.

(3) Three concrete gaps surfaced. Gap A — work_tickets are not being authored when Claude Code prompts get committed: the work_ticket entity is fully defined (governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md, status lifecycle drafted to ready to consumed) and explicitly cites CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md files as canonical examples, but no Claude Code prompts authored after the v0.7 backfill have been recorded as work_ticket database rows. Gap B — no resolves relationship_kind for planning_items; resolution_reference column NULL on every row. Gap C — git commits are not first-class; they live only as text in session.artifacts_produced.

(4) Options framed at three scope levels. Option I (resolves only) — cheapest, does not satisfy the audit goal. Option II (wire existing entities plus add resolution) — middle scope, uses the existing work_ticket entity to make the planning_item to work_ticket to conversation to close_out_payload to deposit_event to planning_item-resolved chain machine-readable end-to-end without modeling git commits. Option III (Option II plus first-class commits) — comprehensive, adds the commit entity and git-log ingestion to make the planning_item to commit-SHA chain queryable in a single SQL join.

(5) Decisions. DEC-168 adopt Option III on Doug's rationale that integration burden grows with delay. DEC-169 methodology-first sequencing — methodology document drives schema additions, not vice versa. DEC-170 seven-planning-item workstream decomposition.

(6) Deferred decisions list named for the methodology conversation (PI-027): commit identifier strategy (CM-NNN sequence vs SHA-as-identifier); granularity of resolves (per-commit vs per-conversation vs both); commit ingestion trigger (manual command vs git post-commit hook vs background polling); commit record fields (sha, message, author, committed_at, repository, branch, parent_sha, files_changed_count, plus any others); multi-repo scoping (commits to ClevelandBusinessMentoring repo vs commits to crmbuilder repo across engagement DBs); typed addresses relationship_kind for work_ticket to planning_item versus stay with generic is_about; work_ticket authoring rule scope (only CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md or all kickoff_prompt-kind documents). Also includes a settle-the-direction call on the blocks relationship_kind, which the v0.7 backfill used in a direction the diagnostic could not unambiguously interpret.

(7) Diagnostic script bugs folded into PI-033 back-fill rather than fixed in isolation, since the script will need re-running against the new schema after PI-029 and PI-030 land anyway.

(8) Kickoff document authored at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/code-change-lifecycle-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md in tight format — goal, deferred decisions, deliverable shape, read list. Full diagnostic walkthrough kept in this session record rather than duplicated into the kickoff. Cannot be authored as a WT-NNN record in this close-out because close-out payloads do not yet support a work_tickets section.

title: Audit feature v1.2 workstream established: planning conversation captured four design decisions, authored audit-v1.2-planning.md, decomposed into 11-prompt Claude Code series
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "I would like to add the ability for the V1 tool to connect to a CRM, and reverse engineer yaml files from selected entities or system information."

Structured summary of the planning conversation:

(1) Codebase investigation revealed the feature already exists. The v1.1 Audit feature shipped on 2026-04-14 (commit 1eabfbb) with full orchestrator, database insertion, classification utilities, worker, sidebar UI, and feature PRD at PRDs/product/features/feat-audit.md marked Status: Implemented. AuditOptions currently exposes six category-level toggles (include_custom_fields, include_native_custom_fields, include_detail_layouts, include_list_layouts, include_relationships, include_native_fields) but no per-entity scope filter. The four metadata-discovery API methods exist on api_client.py: get_all_scopes, get_entity_full_metadata, get_all_links, get_entity_field_list. The InstanceRole enum (source/target/both) gates entry pickers per role.

(2) Three gaps identified for v1.2. Gap one: entity-level scope (no way to audit only the entities that changed). Gap two: filtered tabs (filtered_tab_manager.py exists at 635 lines deploying both halves of the Report Filter plus custom-scope metadata pattern, but audit_manager has no _discover_filtered_tabs). Gap three: security structure (schema v1.3 Section 12 specifies roles, teams, scope access, system permissions, role-aware visibility in over 500 lines, but Section 12 is paper-only — config_loader.py does not reference roles or teams at all, models.py has no RoleDefinition or TeamDefinition dataclass, and no role_manager or team_manager exists in espo_impl/core).

(3) Decision 1 — Users out of audit scope. Three options framed: A (users captured to non-YAML side artifact like users.json), B (schema bump to include user-to-role assignment in YAML), C (users out of scope). Recommended Option C on the rationale that the audit's identity is a YAML-round-trip tool, Section 12 explicitly excludes user assignment from the deployment artifact, and adding non-YAML side output would shift the feature from configuration-capture to configuration-plus-data-snapshot without current operational driver. Doug confirmed Option C: "for now, we are interested in recreating the structure not the user data."

(4) Decision 2 — Roles/teams audit-only versus audit-plus-deploy. Four options framed: A (audit emits, Configure silently no-ops on unknown top-level keys), A-prime (audit emits, Configure treats as NOT_SUPPORTED like saved views/duplicate checks/workflows), B (audit emits plus add role_manager and team_manager deploy-side in same workstream), C (defer security entirely). Initial recommendation was A-prime (matches existing project convention for the three other NOT_SUPPORTED features). Doug overrode toward Option B: "I want to resolve both sides of the issue, so option B."

(5) Decision 2.5 — Scope of Section 12 in the workstream. With Option B committed, the sub-decision was how much of Section 12 to do. Three options: A (all five parts including 12.5 role-aware visibility), B (12.1 through 12.4 plus 12.6, defer 12.5 which requires extending condition_expression.py with a new role: leaf clause variant and threading role predicates through dynamic-logic validators), C (12.1 plus 12.2 only — bare role/team identity). Recommended Option B on the rationale that 12.5 is structurally different work (parser extension versus REST CHECK→ACT) with no current operational driver (zero CBM YAML uses role clauses today). Doug chose Option A: full Section 12 in this workstream.

(6) Decision 3 — Entity picker UX (decided as routine draft, did not pass the two-part test). Pre-flight live discovery versus cached entity list. Pre-flight wins: a single get_all_scopes call adds 1-2 seconds to dialog open; the cached alternative requires client DB schema, invalidation logic, and a manual-refresh UX for no real benefit since a full audit also takes only minutes. Routine choice; recorded as DEC-174 anyway because it is a definite architectural commitment.

(7) Workstream design and series outline. With four decisions settled, decomposed the workstream into 11 Claude Code prompts named audit-v1.2-A through audit-v1.2-K. The series tag mirrors the existing yaml-v1.1 eight-prompt convention. Prompt A: roles/teams top-level recognition plus raw-passthrough dataclasses (loader/models only, no deploy). Prompt B: structured scope_access and system_permissions parsing (Section 12.3 and 12.4). Prompt C: team_manager.py deploy-side CHECK→ACT. Prompt D: role_manager.py deploy-side including scope_access to EspoCRM permission translation — most substantive new module. Prompt E: Section 12.6 deploy ordering plus security pipeline step in run_worker.py. Prompt F: condition_expression.py extension with role: leaf clause variant. Prompt G: Section 12.5 wiring — role-aware requiredWhen/visibleWhen, role-scoped layouts. Prompt H: audit-side _discover_roles, _discover_teams, role-aware logic reversal, security.yaml emission. Prompt I: filtered-tab audit capture. Prompt J: entity-picker UI plus AuditOptions.selected_entities with pre-flight discovery. Prompt K: feat-audit.md bumped to v1.2 plus user-guide updates.

(8) Planning document authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-automation-PRD/audit-v1.2-planning.md (commit ef9799a) is the design authority for the 11-prompt series. Sections: Motivation, Current State Survey (against commit 7e4592f), Design Decisions Captured (Decisions 1, 2, 2.5, 3 with rationale and consequences), Workstream Design End-to-End, Prompt Series Plan with per-prompt dependencies and validation criteria, End State, Out of Scope (including Section 12.7 field-level permissions, user records, general topological deploy ordering, the cross-batch validator-server-state issue, NOT_SUPPORTED resurrection, and v1.1 schema audit coverage extensions), Known Interactions and Risks, and four Open Questions (persona cross-reference validation, empty scope_access warning, defaults for new AuditOptions flags, v1.1-to-v1.2 re-audit overwrite behavior).

(9) Doug approved the planning document as-is, including the provisional answers to the four open questions: persona cross-reference validation = no for v1.2; empty scope_access = warn in audit log only; new AuditOptions flag defaults = False; v1.1-to-v1.2 re-audit = overwrite.

(10) Conversation flow decision. With the planning artifact complete and approved, two natural next moves were flagged: switch to DETAIL mode and write Prompt A in this same session, or close out first and write Prompt A in a fresh conversation. Recommended close-out-first on the rationale that Prompt A authoring is substantial enough to deserve a fresh conversation with full context budget and the close-out is cleaner-scoped to the planning phase alone. Doug confirmed: close out now, Prompt A goes to a fresh conversation.

topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PI-024 prior-workstreams backfill kickoff (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-024-prior-workstreams-backfill-kickoff.md) attached to the conversation's opening turn alongside the project operating-mode preamble. Substantive task: backfill the six prior workstreams pre-dating PI-022 Phase 1 as workstream entity records in the V2 governance database, settle their metadata, and produce the Claude Code prompt that POSTs them.

Structured summary of the planning work:

(1) Identifier rebasing surfaced at orientation. The PI-024 kickoff (authored 05-23-26 05:30) anticipated SES-057 as this conversation's close; in the intervening window SES-057 had been used by the diagnostic-and-design conversation that established the Code Change Lifecycle workstream. Actual identifier heads verified against the CRMBUILDER db-export snapshots: SES-057 highest session, DEC-170 highest decision, WS-001 highest workstream. This conversation therefore closes at SES-059 and produces decisions starting at DEC-175. The kickoff's own re-verification rule anticipated this case.

(2) Contextual note from SES-057. The Code Change Lifecycle workstream queued behind this conversation (PI-027 through PI-033) introduces a first-class commit entity intended to make planning_item-to-commit-SHA traceability queryable via a single SQL join. That sharpens the test for the three questionable candidate workstreams the kickoff names — the YAML schema v1.1 eight-prompt series, the error-handling-prompt series A–E, and the layout-fix triplet of commits in May. These are structurally code-change clusters rather than multi-conversation arcs; the commit entity is being built to index exactly this kind of work.

(3) DEC-175 — Definitive workstream inventory. Three options framed: Option A (strict six clear candidates), Option B (six plus YAML schema v1.1 only — the one questionable candidate with a written master plan), Option C (all nine candidates). Adopted Option A. Two reasons: (a) the kickoff's inclusion test ("coherent purpose, multiple connected conversations or prompts, recognizable beginning/end") is cleanly satisfied by the six clear candidates but only marginally by the three questionable ones; (b) the Code Change Lifecycle workstream is being scoped explicitly to capture commit-level audit trails, and the three questionable candidates are exactly the kind of code-change cluster its first-class commit entity is designed to index — backfilling them as workstreams now creates structure that PI-027 through PI-033 will provide more naturally.

(4) DEC-176 — Lifecycle-date reconstruction strategy. Three candidate sources framed: Option A (git-commit range), Option B (session-date range — the calendar of the workstream's conversation arc), Option C (document-timestamp range — kickoff or master-plan Last Updated for started_at, closeout payload session_date for completed_at). Adopted Option B as the primary rule with Option C as a documented fallback for workstreams lacking clean session bookends. Two reasons: (a) WS-001's precedent effectively uses session-date range (its workstream_started_at approximates SES-047's session_date); staying consistent with the one prior record is worthwhile; (b) the semantic fit is best — workstreams are conversation arcs, and session_date is the conversation arc's calendar. The Option C fallback handles the one or two workstreams where the rule degrades (WS-002 catalog ingestion uses Option C for started_at because planning preceded the single build session by ~5 days; WS-006 CBM paper test uses Option C throughout because its sessions live in the CBM engagement database, not CRMBUILDER's).

(5) Specific dates approved. WS-002 Catalog ingestion 2026-05-09 → 2026-05-14 (Option C started, Option B completed). WS-003 Methodology entity schema design 2026-05-11 → 2026-05-15 (both Option B, SES-011 → SES-024). WS-004 UI v0.5 engagement management 2026-05-16 → 2026-05-17 (both Option B, SES-025 → SES-035). WS-005 UI v0.6 styling 2026-05-16 → 2026-05-18 (both Option B, SES-027 → SES-043). WS-006 CBM paper test 2026-05-18 → 2026-05-19 (both Option C — kickoff Last Updated → findings Last Updated). WS-007 Multi-tenancy routing fix 2026-05-19 → 2026-05-20 (both Option B, SES-044 → SES-045). All timestamps stored as YYYY-MM-DDT00:00:00 (UTC midnight) since wall-clock precision is not available for these backdated records.

(6) WS-006 cross-engagement reading settled. The CBM paper-test conversation sessions live in the CBM engagement database, not CRMBUILDER's — SES-046's session record explicitly cites PI-001 in the Cleveland Business Mentors engagement as the paper-test's blocking finding, confirming the conversations ran in CBM. Read A (workstream lives in CRMBUILDER per kickoff intent; PI-025 handles the cross-engagement conversation-membership question) adopted over Read B (workstream lives in CBM; exclude from PI-024). Read A aligns with the kickoff's listing of CBM paper test among the six to backfill in this engagement and frames the paper test as a CRMBUILDER-dogfood-methodology activity that executed its sessions in CBM for a meaningful validation. The cross-engagement conversation-membership question is deferred to PI-025.

(7) Routine decisions decided per ARCHITECTURE mode and announced. Status for all six workstreams: complete (no exceptions; the CBM paper test completed its validation scope, and the blocking finding it surfaced is a downstream schema-amendment consequence rather than an indication of incompletion). No workstream_supersedes edges: each workstream satisfied its own scope (the v0.4 → v0.5 → v0.6 chain is a release-version sequence, not supersession; methodology entity schema design and governance entity schema design cover different entity types, neither superseded the other).

(8) DEC-177 — Master-plan reference_book bundling. Three options framed: Option A (bundle all six master-plan reference_books, with judgment-call source-document picks for split-document workstreams), Option B (bundle only the three clean cases — methodology, v0.5, v0.6 — defer the three split-document cases), Option C (defer all six to a later phase). Adopted Option B. Three reasons: (a) follows WS-001's established pattern without forcing pick-one judgments on workstreams that genuinely have multi-document planning material; (b) surfaces the multi-document-planning design question as its own future decision rather than answering it implicitly through PI-024's pick choices; (c) keeps PI-024's prompt scope manageable — three RB records and three edges is a small additive change, whereas six RBs with three needing judgment-call source documents would noticeably complicate the apply script and add review burden.

(9) Three reference_book records specified. RB-011 methodology entity schema-design workstream plan (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schema-workstream-plan.md, v1.0 dated 2026-05-11T16:00:00). RB-012 user-interface v0.5 engagement-management workstream plan (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-engagement-management-workstream-plan.md, v1.0 dated 2026-05-16T14:00:00). RB-013 user-interface v0.6 styling workstream plan (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-workstream-plan.md, v1.0 dated 2026-05-16T14:00:00). All kind workstream_master_plan, status active. Three workstream_planned_in_reference_book edges: WS-003 → RB-011, WS-004 → RB-012, WS-005 → RB-013.

(10) Full names, purposes, descriptions, and notes for all six workstream records approved. The full text matches what the backfill script's _WORKSTREAMS list embeds verbatim, so the script file itself is the canonical record of the approved content.

(11) Backfill prompt authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-024-prior-workstreams-backfill.md installs the backfill script, runs it, verifies counts and lifecycle timestamps, and commits in two steps (script-file commit first, then regenerated db-export snapshots commit). Mirrors backfill_governance_phase_1.py's idempotency contract (HTTP 409 and 422-duplicate treated as already-present).

(12) Close-out payload and apply prompt produced. This payload (ses_059.json) and its apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-059.md) cover the three governance decisions only. The workstream and reference_book records arrive out-of-band via the backfill prompt; this payload's records_summary notes that explicitly. Apply order: SES-059 close-out first (lands DEC-175, DEC-176, DEC-177), then the backfill prompt (lands WS-002..007, RB-011..013, three master-plan edges).

title: audit-v1.2 planning resolved: §9 open questions and security.yaml placement decided, planning doc v1.1 through v1.3 authored; rebased from SES-059 to SES-060 to clear PI-024 collision
topics_covered:
Seed: Doug uploaded audit-v1.2-planning.md v1.0 (the kickoff product of SES-058) for review and continuation. No accompanying user message text; the project's standing system prompt set ARCHITECTURE mode and provided the close-out protocol.

Structured summary of the resolution conversation:

(1) Initial review surfaced three architectural concerns not captured in v1.0. First: Prompt H referenced 'Alembic-style migration' for the new role and team client-schema tables. The project does not use Alembic; the actual mechanism is a homegrown versioned migration runner at automation/db/migrations.py with a schema_version table and existing _client_v1 through _client_v3 migrations. The fix is a new _client_v4 migration following the existing pattern. Second: §4.1 established security.yaml as a distinct artifact separate from per-entity YAMLs but did not pin where in a program file's directory structure it lives — root alongside per-entity YAMLs versus a security/ subdirectory. This affects both audit emission (Prompt H) and loader discovery (Prompt A). Third: §8.5 named the 11-prompt series-size risk but proposed no mitigation. Option worth considering: pause at Prompt E for a deploy-side validation milestone before starting the audit-side work in F through K. Doug approved working through the §9 open questions one at a time, with the three concerns flagged for incorporation.

(2) §9.3 — Default for include_security and include_filtered_tabs. Passed the two-part test (operator-facing impact; two viable defaults). Concrete example: Doug's next CBM audit after v1.2 ships, opening the dialog and seeing the new checkboxes in either checked or unchecked state. Options: default-on (consistent with existing AuditOptions pattern where every category-wide option defaults True) versus default-off (new capabilities opt-in to avoid surprising operators). Recommendation: default-on, on the rationale that the audit's identity is capture-everything and a default-off new capability is a quiet inconsistency in that identity with a bad failure mode (operators who never notice the new checkboxes get half-audits indefinitely). The v1.0 provisional default-False was overcautious for the actual operator situation: Doug is effectively the sole operator and will read the v1.2 release notes. Doug approved default-on. This is a revision from v1.0's provisional answer.

(3) §9.4 — Backward compatibility for existing audit YAML. Passed the two-part test (real risk of losing prior audit output; three genuinely different options). Concrete example: Doug audited CBM under v1.1 four weeks ago and got 8 per-entity YAMLs in audits/cbm-source/. What happens when v1.2 runs against the same source? Options: overwrite (latest snapshot replaces prior), alongside (versioned suffix or dated subdirectory, both versions coexist), merge (parse existing files and diff). Recommendation: overwrite with a one-line UX guard — the Audit dialog displays a pre-run confirmation when the output directory contains files matching the audit emission pattern. The merge option was rejected on maintenance grounds (diffing structural YAML is fragile across schema evolution); the alongside option was rejected because two YAML versions of the same entity coexisting in the same program root creates real deploy ambiguity. Doug approved overwrite with the confirmation guard.

(4) §9.1 and §9.2 resolved as routine decisions in a single turn (neither passed the two-part test). §9.1 persona cross-reference validation: kept as documentation metadata only, no validation. Cross-checking would require a source-of-truth list of Master PRD personas, which doesn't live anywhere the loader has access to and would couple the loader to client-specific PRD content. The failure mode of a typo'd persona name is documentation-reader-briefly-confused, not severe enough to justify the infrastructure or coupling. §9.2 empty scope_access: emit informational warning in the audit log when a captured role has no per-entity access. Matches existing audit log pattern for analogous edge cases (e.g., entity with no custom fields); gives operators visibility into roles that grant nothing on the source. Doug approved both.

(5) Planning doc v1.1 authored and pushed (commit 315801b). v1.1 rewrites §9 from open-question subsections to resolved-decision subsections, adds the Alembic→_client_v4 correction to Prompt H and threads the same migration version into Prompt I (one _client_v4 migration covers all v1.2 audit schema additions rather than separate versions per prompt), wires the §9.4 overwrite-confirmation dialog into §4.1 operator flow and Prompt J, and updates §10 to record that §9 is resolved while surfacing two workflow items (series-size mitigation and security.yaml placement) that were not yet decided.

(6) security.yaml file placement decision raised separately as the one remaining design question affecting Prompt A. Passed the two-part test (real loader-discovery and future-proofing impact; two genuinely different options). Concrete example: Doug audits CBM source under v1.2 with security enabled — output ends up either flat (audits/cbm-source/security.yaml alongside per-entity YAMLs at root) or grouped (audits/cbm-source/security/security.yaml under a dedicated subdirectory). Options: root (flat program directory, loader scans *.yaml at root) versus security/ subdirectory (loader scans root and security/ for *.yaml, future security-adjacent files anchor in the same folder). Recommendation: subdirectory, on the rationale that §7 already names v1.4 deferred work (Section 12.7 field-level permissions and permission presets) that will produce additional security-related files; doing it now costs a small loader convention; doing it later costs an output migration for any program previously audited under v1.2. Doug approved subdirectory.

(7) Planning doc v1.2 authored and pushed (commit c9ebcda). v1.2 threads the security/ subdirectory placement through §4.1 step 8 (emission path), §4.1 step 5 and §9.4 consequence (overwrite-confirmation trigger now matches *.yaml at root OR security/*.yaml under the subdirectory), Prompt A's loader scan (root plus security/), Prompt H's emission target, and Prompt J's overwrite-trigger pattern. §10 is trimmed to remove the resolved placement bullet, leaving only the series-size workflow question as deliberately uncaptured in the doc.

(8) Identifier collision discovered and rebased. After v1.2 pushed (15:28 UTC), the close-out payload was authored as ses_059.json with DEC-175..179. On the next pull-rebase before push, origin/main had advanced to commit 44182d1 (Doug pushed at 15:32 UTC) — the PI-024 prior-workstreams backfill conversation had run concurrently in another sandbox and pushed its own SES-059 close-out first, with DEC-175 through DEC-177 covering inventory, lifecycle-date strategy, and master-plan reference_book bundling. The pull-rebase silently overwrote this conversation's ses_059.json with PI-024's version. Resolution: rebase this conversation's records to SES-060 with DEC-178..182, re-author ses_060.json with content identical to the lost ses_059.json apart from identifier shifts, and bump the planning doc to v1.3 to fix the §10 reference 'SES-059 (DEC-175 through DEC-179)' to 'SES-060 (DEC-178 through DEC-182)'. The v1.3 bump is a pure rebase with no design content change.

(9) Series-size mitigation (§8.5) deliberately not resolved. Whether to pause at Prompt E for a deploy-side validation milestone before starting Prompts F through K is a workflow choice for Doug to make at execution time, not a design decision that belongs in the planning document. The doc neither requires nor forbids the pause. Surfaced in §10 for visibility but left to Doug.

(10) Next-conversation kickoff. The next conversation opens against the planning document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-automation-PRD/audit-v1.2-planning.md (now at v1.3) and produces CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-audit-v1.2-A-roles-teams-recognition.md as the PI-034 deliverable.

(11) Lesson learned about concurrent sandbox identifier allocation. The next-available-identifier check against the engagement's db-export snapshot is necessary but not sufficient when multiple Claude.ai sandboxes are running concurrently. Both this conversation and the PI-024 backfill conversation observed the same head (SES-058 from the snapshot, with SES-058's apply not yet run locally, so functionally SES-057 was the latest applied head) and independently allocated SES-059. The collision was only detected at push time via pull-rebase. Out of scope for this conversation but flagged as a methodology issue worth tracking — the planning kickoff for the Code Change Lifecycle workstream (SES-057, PI-027 through PI-033) may be the natural home for this concern since it owns the commit-and-deposit-event entity work.

conversation_reference: Claude.ai planning conversation. Opened against the kickoff document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/code-change-lifecycle-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md, authored by predecessor SES-057. The kickoff established the Code Change Lifecycle workstream (per DEC-168 adopting Option III, first-class commit entities) and named eight design decisions deferred for settlement in this conversation: commit identifier strategy, granularity of resolves edges, commit ingestion trigger, commit record fields, multi-repo and multi-engagement scoping, typed addresses relationship_kind, work_ticket authoring rule scope, and direction/name of the blocks relationship_kind. The conversation ran in ARCHITECTURE mode (project default) and worked through the eight decisions in sequence using the consequential decision template, with each decision recommendation grounded in the actual state of the dogfood database (db-export snapshots queried via Python directly; no MCP server in this sandbox). Five of eight decisions ran full 8-element templates (#1 #2 #3 #5 #8); three were decide-and-announce because the options did not pass part-two of the two-part test (#4 commit fields, #6 addresses typed kind, #7 work_ticket scope rule — though #7 was settled with strong reference to the existing work_ticket spec's pre-authored canonical examples). Three artifacts produced: the methodology document, the PI-028 commit schema kickoff, and the close-out payload itself. The PI-027 resolution posture was settled in passing as option (a) — defer to PI-033 retroactive resolve — to keep PI-027 honest as a normative-but-not-yet-implemented planning item, preserving the diagnostic signal of 'planning items in Resolved status without resolves edges are data-integrity violations.'
