The next conversation opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-commit.md to produce PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/commit.md per the seven-section governance schema spec format. PI-028 stays Open at the end of that conversation (same payload-format-gap reason as PI-027). After PI-028 closes, PI-029 schema migration work begins as a Claude Code execution series; the first prompt CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-029-A-commits-table-and-vocab.md is authored by PI-028's close-out or by a fresh planning conversation depending on PI-028's time-and-context budget. Subsequent planning items PI-030 (close-out payload extension and apply script update and enumerate_commits helper), PI-031 (Commits panel UI), PI-032 (methodology rollout including CLAUDE.md updates), PI-033 (historical backfill including this PI-027 retroactive resolve) execute in strict dependency order per DEC-170. Out-of-scope items deferred per methodology section 1: workstreams that produce no commits stay outside the lifecycle's audit-chain scope; cross-engagement commit queries beyond the per-engagement boundary stay deferred unless a use case surfaces.
title: Code Change Lifecycle methodology drafted: eight deferred design decisions settled, methodology document v1.0 authored, PI-028 commit entity schema kickoff produced
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: kickoff document at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/code-change-lifecycle-workstream-establishing-kickoff.md, opening this conversation against PI-027 to draft methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md and settle eight deferred design decisions, with the PI-028 commit entity schema kickoff as the closing deliverable.

Structured summary of the methodology drafting conversation:

(1) Identifier collision and three-step renumbering. The kickoff was authored by SES-057 anticipating SES-058 and DEC-171..178 for this conversation. The first rebase came when a parallel sandbox completed the audit feature v1.2 planning workstream and queued ses_058.json (claiming SES-058 + DEC-171..174 + PI-034..044); this conversation rebased to SES-059 + DEC-175..182. The second rebase came on push attempt — a parallel sandbox completed the PI-024 prior-workstreams backfill planning workstream and pushed first as ses_059.json (claiming SES-059 + DEC-175..177); this conversation rebased to SES-060 + DEC-178..185. The third rebase came on the next push attempt — yet another parallel sandbox completed the audit-v1.2 §9 resolutions conversation and pushed ses_060.json (claiming SES-060 + DEC-178..182); this conversation rebased to SES-061 + DEC-183..190. The final identifier assignments survived push verification, and applies must run in this order: SES-058 audit feature; SES-059 PI-024 backfill; SES-060 audit-v1.2 resolutions; SES-061 this conversation. Each apply requires its predecessors. The renumbering experience itself surfaces a methodology question for PI-032 to address: parallel-sandbox conversations cannot reserve identifier ranges in advance; reserve-at-apply-time is the only collision-free option. Doug acknowledged each rebase by proceeding.

(2) Decision 1 — commit identifier strategy. Three options framed: (a) CM-NNN sequence with SHA stored separately, (b) SHA-as-identifier with full 40-char SHA as primary key, (c) both — CM-NNN as identifier with SHA as unique secondary key plus /by-sha/{sha} lookup endpoint. Recommended option (a) refined to four-digit padding (CM-NNNN matching REF-NNNN's high-volume pattern) with the /by-sha/{sha} convenience endpoint borrowed from option (c). Rationale: uniformity with the rest of the governance vocabulary (SES-NNN, DEC-NNN, PI-NNN, WT-NNN, WS-NNN, REF-NNNN) beats direct git navigability; the convenience endpoint takes the friction out of the only direction (SHA-to-CM-NNNN) that matters operationally. Doug confirmed.

(3) Decision 2 — granularity of resolves edges. Three options framed: (a) per-commit only, (b) per-conversation only, (c) both. Recommended option (b): single resolves kind for conversation-to-planning_item, with multi-conversation work handled via a separate addresses kind (extended to conversation-to-planning_item as well as work_ticket-to-planning_item in Decision 6). Rationale: per-commit grain forces an authoring judgment Doug should not have to make at close-out time ('did this kickoff commit really contribute or just the methodology commit?'); per-conversation grain matches the natural unit at which resolution is decided and at which planning_item.status transitions. The two-join SQL for 'commits that resolved PI-NNN' is operationally fine. Unique constraint on (target_type, target_id, relationship_kind='resolves') ensures a planning_item is resolved by at most one conversation. Doug confirmed.

(4) Decision 3 — commit ingestion trigger. Four options framed: (a) manual payload enumeration, (b) git post-commit hook, (c) background polling, (d) apply-time git scan. Surfaced a fifth option (a-prime): payload-declared with mechanical enumeration helper at close-out authoring time — combines auditability of (a) with no-miss quality of (d). Recommended (a-prime): the close-out payload contains a commits array enumerating each commit by SHA; a helper command (tools/governance/enumerate_commits.py or inline 'git log --pretty' snippet) runs at close-out authoring time to populate the section from 'git log <last-applied-sha>..HEAD' per touched repo. No hooks, no polling, no apply-time scanning. The 'last applied SHA' is derived per repo by querying GET /commits?commit_repository=<name>&sort=commit_committed_at:desc&limit=1; no separate state table needed. Apply-script idempotency: UNIQUE on commit_sha rejects duplicates with HTTP 409. Doug confirmed.

(5) Decision 4 — commit record fields. Did not pass two-part test (impact real but per-field options are 'include or defer'; outcomes don't materially differ). Decided and announced as a batch: v0.8 fields are commit_identifier, commit_sha (UNIQUE), commit_message_first_line, commit_message_full, commit_author_name, commit_author_email, commit_committed_at (from %cI), commit_repository, commit_branch, commit_parent_shas (JSON array — replaces kickoff's singular 'parent_sha' to handle merge commits cleanly), commit_files_changed_count, commit_conversation_id (FK), plus governance-standard timestamps. Deferred to v0.9: commit_files_changed_paths, commit_signed_by, commit_committer_name/email, commit_message_trailers. The 'parent_shas as JSON array' refinement of the kickoff's singular 'parent_sha' was a side recommendation accepted in the same turn. Doug confirmed.

(6) Decision 5 — multi-repo and multi-engagement scoping. Four options framed: (a) repo-scoped commits with cross-engagement references, (a-prime) conversation-scoped commits (all live in the conversation's engagement DB with repository column distinguishing physical repo), (b) per-engagement child records, (c) shared meta-DB. Recommended (a-prime). Rationale: engagements are accounting units; every commit lives with its conversation; no cross-engagement references for commits; no new shared-DB pattern. Edge case named: a CBM-engagement conversation that incidentally commits in crmbuilder (rare — dogfood scope but possible) lives in CBM engagement DB regardless. PI-033 back-fill scope: each historical commit assigned to its historical conversation, which lives in one engagement; the commit goes in that engagement. A cross-engagement 'where is this SHA?' lookup utility is cheap (iterate the commits tables of each engagement DB) but deferred until a use case surfaces. Doug confirmed.

(7) Decision 6 — typed addresses relationship_kind. Did not pass two-part test (the functional outcomes of 'addresses' vs 'is_about with type filters' don't materially differ; only the query-clarity reading differs). Decided and announced. Option (a): add addresses as a typed relationship_kind admitting two source/target pairs — (work_ticket, planning_item) (the kickoff's narrow case) and (conversation, planning_item) (the multi-conversation case from Decision 2). Many-to-many cardinality on both sides. Existing v0.7 backfill's eight work_ticket-to-planning_item is_about edges migrate to addresses as part of PI-033. DEC-133's frequency-justified deferral applies to speculative vocabulary; addresses has named query patterns and a committed use site in this workstream, so deferral test does not trigger. Doug confirmed.

(8) Decision 7 — work_ticket authoring rule scope. Three options framed: (a) narrow (Claude Code prompts only, ~168 historical), (b) middle (Claude Code plus kickoff/planning-prompt files, ~195 historical), (c) broad (every single-use seed file including SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-*, ~221 historical). Recommended (c) broad. Rationale: the work_ticket spec's work_ticket_kind closed enum has values kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other — designed for all three categories; the spec's canonical-examples narrative names files from all three categories; the spec author intent was already broad. Filename pattern classifies: CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md → claude_code_prompt; *-kickoff*.md and *-planning-prompt*.md → kickoff_prompt; SESSION-PROMPT-* and UPDATE-PROMPT-* → ad_hoc_prompt; other → other. Back-fill cost: ~213 net new records under (c) versus ~160 under (a) — half-day to one-day difference; not material. Going-forward enforcement is documented in PI-032's methodology rollout. Doug confirmed.

(9) Decision 8 — direction and name of the blocks relationship_kind. Three options framed: (a) keep blocks with 'source blocks target = target waits for source' convention (natural English; existing two rows must be swapped to match), (b) keep blocks with inverted convention (existing rows match as-is; future authors learn the inverted convention), (c) rename blocks to blocked_by (existing rows keep source/target endpoints; only kind value migrates; semantics match natural English with no convention to memorize). Recommended (c) blocked_by. Rationale: a self-documenting kind name removes the need for future readers to consult the methodology to interpret an edge; conventions that disagree with natural English get violated in the long run. The two existing rows REF-0357 (source=PI-025 target=PI-024) and REF-0358 (source=PI-026 target=PI-025) keep endpoints; their kind value migrates from 'blocks' to 'blocked_by'. Folded into PI-029's Alembic migration. Doug confirmed.

(10) PI-027 resolution posture. Per kickoff section 'Close-out expectations,' two options for handling this conversation's own resolution given the payload format gap: (a) defer (leave PI-027 Open in this close-out; PI-033 resolves it retroactively once resolves_planning_items payload section ships in PI-030), or (b) hand-flip PI-027's status via direct API after apply. Methodology section 9 adopts (a). Rationale: the honesty of 'the methodology specifies a model the schema does not yet support' matters more than the visual neatness of a flipped status; the diagnostic that motivated the workstream ('PI-022 is the only Resolved planning item, and it has no resolves edge') would be impossible to detect under (b); adopting (a) preserves the diagnostic signal through this conversation's tail.

(11) Methodology document authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md v1.0 (this conversation) is the design authority for the seven-stage Code Change Lifecycle chain. Nine sections: Purpose and Scope; the Seven-Stage Lifecycle (planning item → planning conversation → execution conversation opens against work_ticket → execution conversation produces commits → close-out records resolves/addresses/work_tickets/commits → apply transaction lands records → downstream queries walk chain); Vocabulary Additions (commit entity type, resolves and addresses kinds, blocks→blocked_by rename); Close-out Payload Format Extension (four new sections: commits, work_tickets, resolves_planning_items, addresses_planning_items; fixed apply order); Authoring Rules (when work_ticket is born/consumed/superseded; when planning_item is resolved; commit ingestion at close-out time; attribution rule for manual commits between close-outs); Audit Query Patterns (five canonical SQL examples covering PI→commits, commit→PIs, workstream→commits, indeterminate-state detection, work_ticket→PIs); Migration and Back-fill Posture (PI-033 scope: ~221 historical work_tickets, several hundred commits, retroactive resolves edges, two is_about-to-addresses migrations, two blocks-to-blocked_by rows); Known Gaps and Downstream Dependencies (PI-028 through PI-033 mapped to deliverables; methodology is normative-but-not-yet-implemented until PI-030 ships); PI-027 Resolution Posture (option a — defer).

(12) PI-028 commit entity schema kickoff authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-commit.md is the work_ticket seeding the PI-028 schema-design conversation. The kickoff intentionally defers most architectural questions to the methodology document and surfaces only the rendering-into-schema-spec-format questions: lifecycle posture (documentary), cross-spec precedent contributions (three candidates: by-natural-key lookup pattern, JSON array column for variable-cardinality scalars, conversation-scoped accounting unit principle), field naming convention reaffirmation (parent-prefix per DEC-046), validation rules for natural-key columns (commit_sha 40-hex-lowercase, commit_repository non-empty), relationship rendering (commit_conversation_id as FK only, no typed refs kind per DEC-133 deferral test), acceptance criteria specific to commit, and derived endpoint candidates. topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Project default operating mode: ARCHITECTURE." [project preamble], followed by the PI-028 kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-commit.md attached to the opening turn. Substantive task: author PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/commit.md per the seven-section format defined in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, settling the seven design questions enumerated in the kickoff.

Structured summary of the schema-design work:

(1) Identifier rebasing at orientation, then again at close. The kickoff (authored 05-23-26 21:00 by SES-061) anticipated this conversation closing at SES-061 itself per the kickoff close-out-expectations block. In the intervening window SES-060 (audit-v1.2 planning, DEC-178..182) and SES-061 (methodology drafting, DEC-183..190) had been authored and their payloads queued in close-out-payloads/. At conversation start, actual heads verified against the CRMBUILDER db-export snapshots showed SES-059 as highest applied session and ses_060.json / ses_061.json queued, so this conversation planned to close at SES-062 with decisions DEC-191..193. During close-out artifact authoring, a final pull surfaced that a parallel sandbox session had already pushed ses_062.json (PI-025 prior-conversations backfill, DEC-191..197) and its apply prompt; the close-out rebased a second time to SES-063 and DEC-198..200. The narrative and the renamed files (ses_063.json, CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-063.md) reflect the second rebase. The apply prompt's pre-flight re-verifies against the live API in case a third concurrent close-out arrives between now and apply time.

(2) Pre-flight reading. CLAUDE.md (root), methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md v1.0, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md v1.0, governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md v1.0, governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md v1.1, and crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py read end-to-end before drafting. The other four governance schema specs (workstream, conversation, close_out_payload, deposit_event) spot-checked for cross-spec precedent context.

(3) Plan articulated and approved before drafting. Per ARCHITECTURE mode, the conversation laid out how each of the seven kickoff design questions would be handled and surfaced the calls that genuinely needed Doug's input. All seven decided autonomously per ARCHITECTURE mode's decide-and-announce posture; none individually passed the two-part stop-the-flow test (real downstream impact AND two viable options with meaningfully different outcomes), as the methodology document had already pre-decided the architectural shape and the remaining work was rendering decisions into the standard format.

(4) DEC-198 — Status-free documentary lifecycle. The methodology's v0.8 field inventory (§3.1) has no commit_status column. The reference_book spec (DEC-137 source) established that documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only, but reference_book still carries a three-value status field (active → archived / superseded). Commit goes further: no status field at all. The spec articulates this as a refinement of DEC-137 — documentary lifecycles MAY drop the status field entirely when no operationally meaningful states exist — and frames it as MAY-not-MUST so future documentary entities with even a two-status distinction continue to follow reference_book's precedent.

(5) DEC-199 — FK-over-references-edge deviation. The methodology §3.1 chose a foreign-key column (commit_conversation_id) for the commit→conversation relationship, and the audit queries in methodology §6.1 and §6.2 JOIN through the FK directly. This deviates from DEC-124's cross-spec precedent (references-edge over FK for parent-child governance relationships). The spec articulates the deviation explicitly with three justifications: frequency of traversal (every audit query walks commit→conversation; JOIN cost compounds linearly with commit count), cardinality (every commit has exactly one producing conversation, which FK + NOT NULL expresses cleanly), and DEC-187 reinforcement (the conversation-scoped accounting unit principle makes ownership mandatory by the model; FK enforces mandatory ownership at the schema level). The deviation is framed as the sibling of DEC-133's frequency-justified-deferral logic — the same volume-of-traversal reasoning, applied in the opposite direction.

(6) DEC-200 — Four new cross-spec precedents. The spec elevates four precedents successor governance specs may inherit: (a) status-free documentary lifecycles (per DEC-198's refinement of DEC-137); (b) natural-key lookup endpoints via GET /{plural}/by-{key}/{value} — introduced by GET /commits/by-sha/{sha} for SHA-based lookup, generalizes to any future entity with a strong natural key distinct from its V2 identifier; (c) JSON-array columns for variable-cardinality scalar lists — introduced by commit_parent_shas (0/1/2 elements), generalizes to any 0-to-N scalar list intrinsic to the entity's identity that doesn't warrant a separate normalized table; (d) conversation-scoped accounting unit principle — restating DEC-187 (settled by methodology authoring at SES-061) in spec-inheritable form. The fourth is the most consequential structurally because it generalizes the engagement-as-accounting-unit principle to any future entity type representing external artifacts (a network response, a deployed config, a database backup).

(7) Routine design choices decided per ARCHITECTURE mode and announced. Four-digit identifier width CM-NNNN (vs the three-digit governance default of CM-NNN), justified by expected cardinality (~700 historical + ongoing development volume). Client-side identifier assignment per CLAUDE.md line 62 (deviation from spec-guide §6's server-side default, consistent with every other prefixed-identifier entity in V2). SHA validation as ^[0-9a-f]{40}$ with lowercase enforced; SHA-256 migration anticipation captured as §3.8.2 open question. Repository validation: non-empty trimmed, no whitespace, no path separators, no scheme prefix; no enum constraint (new repos added as encountered). commit_committed_at preserves ISO 8601 offset (deviation from base UTC) because committer-local time is informational signal. Soft-delete-with-restore preferred over born-terminal append-only (contrast with deposit_event) because mis-ingestion correction is a real path that born-terminal would forbid. Commits panel under Governance sidebar group with Repository column and Repository filter combo (natural addition paralleling work_ticket's Kind column and close_out_payload's Status filter combo precedents); default sort commit_committed_at descending (audit-log convention paralleling deposit_event's deviation from V2's default ascending).

(8) Thirteen acceptance criteria captured in §3.7, above the spec-guide §7.1 floor of ten.

(9) Five open questions captured in §3.8 with explicit category tags: derived endpoint URL shapes [for build-planning], SHA-256 migration anticipation [for retroactive backfill or future user-interface version], commit_author_kind attribution discipline [for future user-interface version], branch-pinning queries [for retroactive backfill], sub-grouping in the Governance sidebar [for build-planning].

(10) PI-029 Prompt A authored at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-029-A-commits-table-and-vocab.md. Scope: one Alembic migration (revision 0012) adding the commits table, extending refs.source_type/target_type CHECKs to admit 'commit', updating refs.relationship_kind CHECK in two steps (interim admits both 'blocks' and 'blocked_by'; data migration UPDATEs the two REF-0357/REF-0358 rows; final removes 'blocks'), extending change_log.entity_type CHECK; one vocab.py update adding 'commit' to ENTITY_TYPES, adding 'resolves' / 'addresses' / 'blocked_by' to REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS, removing 'blocks', adding the three new _kinds_for_pair clauses, removing the legacy 'blocks'-emitting clauses; three migration-mechanics tests verifying table creation, blocks→blocked_by data migration, and vocab integrity. Subsequent prompts (B access layer, C REST endpoints, D tests) emerge from this prompt's review and a future PI-029 build-planning conversation.

(11) PI-028 stays Open at this conversation's close per methodology §9. The resolves_planning_items payload section the methodology specifies does not yet ship (PI-030 work), so this conversation cannot resolve its own planning item via the new mechanism. PI-033's back-fill resolves PI-028 retroactively. Honesty of "the methodology specifies a model the schema does not yet support" matters more than visual neatness of a flipped status.

(12) Close-out payload and apply prompt produced. This payload (ses_063.json) and its apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-063.md) cover only the three governance decisions and one is_about edge from the session to PI-028; PI-029 Prompt A is committed separately for Doug to execute via Claude Code after this close-out applies.

conversation_reference: Claude.ai conversation opened against the seed prompt 'We need to deploy the CRM Builder V2 api and mcp somewhere where claude.ai can begin accessing the information stored in the database. There are way too many problems where it does not know what is current, or writes prompts that are out of date vs the database.' The conversation ran in ARCHITECTURE mode (project default) and worked through one consequential architectural decision plus three decide-and-announce follow-on decisions. The architectural decision (DEC-201) was framed using the eight-element consequential decision template with three options: (A) local-canonical database with public MCP via tunnel, (B) remote-canonical database on VPS or managed store with desktop UI and Claude.ai both as remote clients, (C) hybrid with stale read-only snapshot pushed to cloud. Option C was dismissed in the framing as not solving the stated problem (claude.ai writing out-of-date prompts because the snapshot it reads is also out of date). Doug approved Option A with the terse acknowledgment 'a'. After approval, the conversation verified the MCP transport against the codebase: cloned the crmbuilder repo with sparse checkout (CLAUDE.md, PRDs/, crmbuilder-v2/), read crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/mcp_server/server.py line 44 to confirm server.run('stdio') and the docstring 'Claude Desktop pipes here', counted 44 tools exposed in tools.py (broad read+write surface including replace_charter, create_decision, delete_reference, etc.), verified mcp>=1.0 dependency (FastMCP supports stdio/sse/streamable-http transports), and checked config.py confirming api_host='127.0.0.1' default. Three decide-and-announce decisions followed: DEC-202 (expose MCP server only, REST API stays localhost — narrows attack surface and matches Doug's single-laptop Claude Code workflow); DEC-203 (single remote MCP with marker-driven engagement routing — matches existing single-engagement-active workflow versus per-engagement tunnel deploys); DEC-204 (Cloudflare Tunnel on subdomain mcp.crmbuilder.com with Cloudflare Access identity gating — requires moving crmbuilder.com nameservers to Cloudflare from current registrar). Doug confirmed the domain selection unprompted ('We have the crmbuilder.com domain registered and it would make more sense to use that. It is not on cloudflair.'). No code changes authored in this conversation; the deliverables are the four governance decisions, the planning item PI-045 that scopes the implementation workstream, and the in-flight follow-on to draft the deployment kickoff prompt in a separate Claude.ai conversation. On push, the payload collided with a parallel sandbox that had pushed ses_062.json first (PI-025 prior-conversations backfill planning); this conversation rebased to SES-064 + DEC-201..201 + PI-045 and re-pushed.

The kickoff prompt sequences: (1) Claude Code prompt for the transport flag and shared-secret middleware in the MCP server; (2) Claude Code prompt addressing the engagement-marker-mid-process risk identified in DEC-203; (3) operational checklist for the Cloudflare nameserver migration, cloudflared launchd service install, named tunnel + Access policy creation, claude.ai MCP URL registration; (4) smoke-test plan for the 44-tool surface; (5) documentation updates to crmbuilder-v2 README and repo CLAUDE.md. The kickoff drafting conversation should open with the four decisions DEC-201..DEC-204 as inputs and produce the kickoff document at a path under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/.
title: V2 remote-access deployment architecture decided: Option A (local-canonical DB + tunneled MCP) approved; transport verified stdio-only; marker-driven engagement routing chosen; tunnel under crmbuilder.com via Cloudflare Tunnel + Access
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "We need to deploy the CRM Builder V2 api and mcp somewhere where claude.ai can begin accessing the information stored in the database. There are way too many problems where it does not know what is current, or writes prompts that are out of date vs the database."

Structured summary of the architectural decision conversation:

(0) Identifier rebase (two collisions). The close-out payload was originally drafted as ses_062.json claiming SES-062 + DEC-191..194 + PI-045 against snapshot heads of SES-061/DEC-190/PI-044. On first push, a parallel sandbox had pushed ses_062.json first (PI-025 prior-conversations backfill planning, DEC-191..197). Rebased to SES-063 + DEC-198..201 + PI-045. On second push, yet another parallel sandbox had pushed ses_063.json first (commit entity schema spec for PI-029, DEC-198..200). Rebased to SES-064 + DEC-201..204 + PI-045 (PIs head unchanged through both rebases because neither colliding payload authored planning items). Predecessor apply chain extended by two: SES-058 → SES-059 → SES-060 → SES-061 → SES-062 (PI-025 backfill planning, parallel sandbox A) → SES-063 (commit entity schema spec, parallel sandbox B) → SES-064 (this conversation). This is the same parallel-sandbox identifier-collision case the Code Change Lifecycle methodology conversation hit twice in SES-061 — three active sandboxes claiming consecutive identifiers within minutes strongly reinforces SES-061's flag that reserve-at-apply-time is the only collision-free model. The PI-032 methodology rollout scope should treat this as a near-term priority rather than a future-tense follow-on.

(1) Problem reframed. The pain Doug named — claude.ai writing prompts that are out of date versus the database — is the inverse of the v2 architectural premise. The database is supposed to be canonical for sessions, decisions, planning items, references; if claude.ai cannot read it, then claude.ai is not operating against canonical state and necessarily produces drift between conversation-memory inferences and reality. The fix is to give claude.ai a read+write path to the canonical store.

(2) Three options framed under the eight-element template. Option A: local-canonical DB with tunneled MCP — laptop stays source of truth, MCP server gets a public URL via tunnel, REST API stays on localhost, claude.ai reaches the MCP whenever the laptop is on and reachable. Option B: remote-canonical DB — move SQLite to a small VPS or migrate to managed DB (Postgres/Turso); desktop UI and claude.ai are both clients of the remote API; always-on but requires desktop UI rework for network latency and an offline story that does not exist today. Option C: hybrid scheduled push of read-only snapshot to cloud — dismissed as not solving the stated problem since stale snapshots produce the exact failure mode Doug is escaping. Why this matters: the v2 premise depends on every actor (desktop, claude.ai, Claude Code) reading and writing the same canonical store; today only two of three can reach it. The choice between A and B determines whether the gap closes by letting claude.ai reach into the laptop, or by moving the canonical store to a place all three actors reach equally. Recommendation: Option A. Cost named honestly: claude.ai data access is bounded by laptop online time; if Doug wants to think through a CBM question on a phone while the laptop is shut, claude.ai gets nothing. For the actual usage pattern (session work in front of the laptop) this is invisible; for ambient queries it is broken. Doug confirmed Option A with 'a'.

(3) Transport verification (post-approval prerequisite). Cloned dbower44022/crmbuilder via sparse checkout (CLAUDE.md, PRDs/, crmbuilder-v2/) at HEAD commit 8215614. Read crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/mcp_server/server.py — line 44 calls server.run('stdio'); the module docstring explicitly notes 'crmbuilder-v2-mcp # talk MCP over stdio (Claude Desktop pipes here)'. The current MCP server is stdio-only and not remotely reachable by claude.ai. Dependency check on pyproject.toml confirms mcp>=1.0 (the official Python SDK whose FastMCP class supports three transports — stdio, sse, streamable-http). Switching is a one-line change at the server.run() call site plus a CLI flag in cli.py:run_mcp. The REST API at config.py:55 binds to api_host='127.0.0.1' by default and stays unchanged — the MCP server speaks to it over localhost. Tool surface counted at 44 tools in tools.py including charter/status/decisions/sessions/risks/planning/topics/references CRUD plus catalog queries; the write surface includes replace_charter, create_decision, update_decision, delete_decision, create_session, replace_status, add_reference, delete_reference, create_risk, update_risk, create_planning_item, update_planning_item, create_topic, update_topic. The auth posture has to assume any caller could rewrite the charter, so Cloudflare Access identity gating at the edge plus a shared-secret header validated by the MCP HTTP transport is the right belt-and-braces pattern.

(4) Complication surfaced: engagement routing. The API is single-engagement per process — cli.py:run_api reads current_engagement.json (written by the desktop UI's Engagements panel) or --engagement flag at launch to pick which database to serve. CBM-engagement work and CRMBUILDER-engagement work read different databases. claude.ai sessions on either workstream need to reach the right one. Two viable options: (X) marker-driven, single remote MCP that follows whichever engagement is currently active in current_engagement.json; (Y) per-engagement deploys — two tunnels, two API+MCP process pairs, two claude.ai MCP URLs. (Y) is architecturally cleaner since claude.ai picks engagement by URL but roughly doubles the operational footprint. (X) matches the existing workflow with zero new state for Doug to manage. DEC-203 chose (X) decide-and-announce. Known footgun named: if Doug switches engagements mid-conversation in the desktop UI, the conversation's claude.ai reads switch silently to the new engagement; the API needs either to detect marker changes on each request or restart on marker change. Mitigation is part of PI-045's scope and is named in the kickoff prompt for the follow-on conversation.

(5) Public surface scope. DEC-202 decide-and-announce: expose the MCP server only via the tunnel, not the REST API. The MCP process talks to the API over localhost unchanged. Rationale: narrows the public attack surface to the MCP protocol; the 44 tools are the contract claude.ai consumes; REST endpoints would be redundant for claude.ai and double the auth surface. The alternative — exposing both — would let Claude Code prompts run remotely too (cross-machine appeal), but Doug runs Claude Code only at the laptop today; the marginal value does not justify the extra surface.

(6) Tunnel product and host. DEC-204 decide-and-announce: Cloudflare Tunnel on subdomain mcp.crmbuilder.com with Cloudflare Access policy gating by identity (Google login or similar) before requests reach the MCP, plus a shared-secret header the MCP validates. Doug confirmed crmbuilder.com is the right host (clearer than dougbower.com for project-scoped infrastructure) and noted it is currently not on Cloudflare — moving nameservers to Cloudflare (free plan, ~15 minutes) is a prerequisite operational step. Alternatives considered: Tailscale Funnel (no DNS migration but ties auth to tailnet membership rather than identity provider); ngrok (free tier unstable hostnames; paid ~$8/mo); raw SSH reverse tunnel (no identity gating). Cloudflare wins on stable hostname + free Access + project-scoped DNS.

(7) Planning item authored. PI-045 V2 remote-access deployment scopes the full workstream: (a) code changes — add --transport {stdio,streamable-http} flag to crmbuilder-v2-mcp CLI, parameterize transport in server.py main(), add shared-secret header middleware to the FastMCP HTTP transport, address the marker-change-mid-process risk identified in DEC-203 (re-read current_engagement.json per request or fail-loud on marker change with restart); (b) operational — move crmbuilder.com nameservers to Cloudflare, install cloudflared as a launchd service on Doug's laptop, create the named tunnel and Access policy for mcp.crmbuilder.com, register the MCP URL in claude.ai's MCP integration; (c) verification — smoke-test all 44 tools end-to-end, confirm auth gating blocks unauthenticated requests at both layers, confirm engagement marker drives the right database; (d) documentation — README section in crmbuilder-v2 covering remote deployment, entry in repo CLAUDE.md, kickoff prompt at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ that opens the implementation conversation. PI-045 status Open at end of this conversation.

(8) In-flight at end. The deployment kickoff prompt that PI-045 will be opened against is NOT authored in this conversation. The kickoff prompt is the deliverable of a follow-on Claude.ai conversation whose seed prompt will be 'draft the V2 remote-access deployment kickoff prompt for PI-045 per the four decisions in SES-064.' This split keeps the architectural decision conversation focused on its purpose and lets the kickoff drafting conversation read the four DECs as inputs.

(9) Sandbox commit and push. The ses_064.json close-out payload and CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-062.md apply prompt are committed and pushed together in one commit per the sandbox push convention.

conversation_reference: Claude.ai conversation opened against the seed prompt 'Open the PI-045 V2 remote-access deployment planning conversation. Read the kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-045-remote-access-deployment-kickoff.md and proceed.' The conversation ran in ARCHITECTURE mode (project default and explicit in the kickoff). The mandate from the kickoff was to (1) surface the engagement-marker-mid-process question identified in DEC-203's named footgun using the eight-element consequential decision template; (2) decide and announce inline the five operational implementation choices below the consequential threshold (HTTP port, header name, secret storage, identity provider, doc home); (3) draft the three Claude Code prompts for the code-changes implementation conversation as drafts presented in a block for Doug's review. Orientation read: crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md and the PI-045 kickoff end-to-end; SES-064 close-out payload for the four anchor decisions DEC-201 through DEC-204; the relevant codebase surface for the marker-handling decision (mcp_server/server.py line 44 confirmed at server.run('stdio'); cli.py run_mcp at line 144 as a thin wrapper, run_api at line 28 with marker resolution; tool count in tools.py verified at 44; mcp>=1.0 in pyproject.toml; FastMCP supports streamable-http transport per the installed SDK version). One codebase finding that materially changed the framing of the consequential decision: route_settings_to_engagement() in runtime/engagement_routing.py is NOT a stat-and-read operation — it mutates os.environ for CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH and CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR, calls reset_settings_cache(), access_db.reset_engine_cache(), and init_meta_db_pool(). The kickoff's framing of Option 1 (per-request marker re-read) as 'small — a stat + a JSON read per request, both cached by the OS page cache' understates the cost: per-request rerouting would reset the SQLAlchemy engine cache on every request (defeating connection pooling) and create a race window under concurrent in-flight requests across a marker flip. The cheap version of silent-follow is a structural refactor of the access layer to thread per-request engagement codes through every DB call rather than reading from singleton Settings — well beyond PI-045's scope. This finding moved the recommendation from a balanced trade-off to a clear preference for fail-loud, and Doug approved (b) fail-loud with the terse 'b'. The five decide-and-announce items were settled as suggested by the kickoff: port 8810 via CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_HTTP_PORT; header X-CRMBuilder-Secret; secret storage via launchd-plist env var sourced from a 0600-mode file at ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret with the canonical value held in Doug's password manager out-of-band, manual three-place rotation; Cloudflare Access Google login matching Doug's owner identity; new documentation file at crmbuilder-v2/docs/remote-deployment.md. Doug approved all three drafts as-is with the terse 'approved'. No identifier rebase was required during the conversation: a pre-commit fetch confirmed two new commits had landed on origin/main since the initial sandbox clone (audit-v1.2 prompts A and B), but the governance snapshots remained at SES-064 / DEC-204 / PI-045, so SES-065 / DEC-205 were clear to claim. The conversation closes with five files committed and pushed together in one commit per the sandbox push convention: the SES-065 close-out payload (this file), the SES-065 apply prompt, and the three PI-045 Claude Code prompts.

title: PI-045 V2 remote-access deployment planning: DEC-205 fail-loud on engagement-marker drift; five operational implementation choices announced inline; three Claude Code prompts authored for the code-changes implementation conversation
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Open the PI-045 V2 remote-access deployment planning conversation. Read the kickoff at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-045-remote-access-deployment-kickoff.md` and proceed."

Structured summary of the planning conversation:

(1) Orientation. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md for engagement context (sparse-checkout pattern, v0.7 governance entity state, working conventions including the sandbox commit-and-push convention). Read the PI-045 kickoff end-to-end including the Surface-and-settle section with the two framed options for the consequential decision and the five decide-and-announce items. Read the SES-064 close-out payload to confirm the four anchor decisions DEC-201 through DEC-204 in full text. Verified identifier heads from PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ at SES-064 / DEC-204 / PI-045 (counts: 63 sessions, 201 decisions, 45 planning items, 634 references); confirmed no payload exists yet for SES-065. Verified codebase facts the kickoff cited: mcp_server/server.py main() calls server.run('stdio') at line 44 with module docstring 'Claude Desktop pipes here'; cli.py has run_mcp at line 144 (thin wrapper) and run_api at line 28 (with marker resolution); tool count in mcp_server/tools.py is 44; pyproject.toml declares mcp>=1.0 (FastMCP supports stdio / sse / streamable-http transports). Minor citation drift noted: the kickoff refers to 'crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/api/config.py' for api_host/api_port; the actual settings module is crmbuilder_v2/config.py. Drift is cosmetic — does not affect scope.

(2) Codebase finding that reframed DEC-205. Read runtime/engagement_routing.py end-to-end to understand the cost of the kickoff's Option 1 (per-request marker re-read). Discovered that route_settings_to_engagement() is NOT a stat-and-read operation: it queries the meta DB to validate the engagement code, mutates os.environ for CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH and CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR, calls reset_settings_cache(), access_db.reset_engine_cache(), and init_meta_db_pool(). Calling this per request would reset the SQLAlchemy engine cache on every dispatch (defeating connection pooling and adding real latency on warm caches) and create a race window under concurrent in-flight requests bracketing a marker flip. The cheap framing in the kickoff ('a stat + a JSON read per request, both cached by the OS page cache after the first call') only gets you detection, not rerouting. The actually-cheap silent-follow option requires a structural refactor of the access layer to thread per-request engagement codes through every DB call rather than reading from singleton Settings — beyond PI-045's scope. This finding moved Option 2 (fail-loud) from a balanced alternative to a clear preference, with the cost of Option 1 named honestly in the surfaced decision.

(3) DEC-205 surfaced using the eight-element consequential decision template. Plain-language question: when Doug flips the engagement marker in the desktop UI mid-conversation, what should the remote MCP API do? Concrete example: CBM decision flow with one decision already written; Doug flips to CRMBUILDER between writes; second decision lands in CRMBUILDER under a CRMBUILDER identifier while Doug's mental model is 'still writing CBM decisions in the same conversation.' Options framed: (a) per-request reroute with cost named at process-global state mutation per request and connection-pool reset; (b) per-request fail-loud with a Starlette/FastAPI middleware that stats the marker file, reads engagement_code, compares to _MARKER_AT_START captured at process start, returns HTTP 409 on mismatch with structured body {error: 'engagement_marker_changed', marker_at_start, marker_now, action: 'restart API to bind to new engagement'} and a WARNING log line. Why it matters: correctness model for engagement binding — per-request (silent follow) versus per-process (engagement is the marker's value at API start, a switch is a visible event). Cost of recommended option named honestly: forces a manual API restart on each engagement switch (~5 seconds of downtime, in-flight requests 409 once); friction roughly invisible for Doug's actual stretch-on-one-engagement pattern; a desktop-UI 'Restart API' button is a natural follow-up if friction surfaces. Recommendation: (b) fail-loud. Doug approved with the terse 'b'.

(4) Five decide-and-announce items settled inline. Each was confirmed as below the two-part consequential test on closer examination (port and header are matching-token choices, secret-storage is conventional macOS practice, identity-provider follows owner identity, doc home follows the kickoff's suggested split). MCP HTTP transport port = 8810, configured via CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_HTTP_PORT to match the existing Settings env-var pattern (REST API stays at 8765; MCP HTTP at 8810 keeps the v2 services adjacent). Shared-secret header name = X-CRMBuilder-Secret, lives identically in three places (laptop env var CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET, the MCP middleware via hmac.compare_digest, claude.ai's MCP integration custom-header configuration). Shared-secret storage = single-line file at ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret (mode 0600), sourced into a launchd-plist-defined env var for the MCP process; canonical value held out-of-band in Doug's password manager; rotation is manual three-place edit (the file, claude.ai's MCP integration, then launchctl unload+load). Cloudflare Access identity provider = Google login, single Access policy on mcp.crmbuilder.com requiring Google auth for Doug's owner email. Documentation home = new file crmbuilder-v2/docs/remote-deployment.md, with the top-level README getting a one-paragraph pointer and crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md getting a Tier-2 paragraph naming the remote MCP URL and the marker-driven routing implication.

(5) Three Claude Code prompts authored for the PI-045 code-changes implementation. Each is a self-contained slice that can be applied in series in one Claude Code session. Slice A (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-045-A-transport-flag-and-http-binding.md): adds --transport {stdio,streamable-http} and --port to the crmbuilder-v2-mcp CLI; adds Settings.mcp_http_port (env CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_HTTP_PORT, default 8810); parameterizes mcp_server.server.main() on transport/host/port with stdio as the default; host hardcoded to 127.0.0.1 per DEC-202 (MCP-only public exposure dialed by cloudflared); tests cover argparse choices and the build-and-dispatch path; no auth in this slice. Slice B (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-045-B-shared-secret-middleware.md): adds Settings.mcp_shared_secret (env CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET, default None); startup hard-fails if streamable-http is selected without the secret; new Starlette SharedSecretMiddleware in a new module mcp_server/middleware.py validates X-CRMBuilder-Secret via hmac.compare_digest and 401s misses before route dispatch; stdio bypasses the middleware entirely; one open question flagged for Claude Code at run-time: how the installed FastMCP SDK version exposes its ASGI app for middleware registration — if no clean path exists in the installed version, prompt B stops and reports rather than skip silently. Slice C (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-045-C-marker-handling.md): implements DEC-205; new module api/marker_guard.py with module-level _MARKER_AT_START and EngagementMarkerGuardMiddleware; cli.run_api() calls set_marker_at_start(active_code) after the existing engagement resolution and before uvicorn.run; the middleware compares the live marker reading to the captured value on every non-exempt request and returns HTTP 409 with the structured body and a WARNING log line on mismatch; exempt paths /health, /openapi.json, /docs, /redoc bypass the guard. Captures active_code (the resolved engagement code that may have come from --engagement flag or the marker file) rather than re-reading the marker, so processes started with --engagement override stay bound to that engagement regardless of later marker changes. Tests cover match / mismatch / missing-marker / exempt-bypass. Drafts were presented via present_files for Doug's review; Doug approved all three with the terse 'approved'.

(6) Identifier rebase check. Pre-commit fetch of origin/main brought in two new commits since the initial sandbox clone (1f7bd0e 'Add audit-v1.2 Prompts A and B' and 84b55c2 'Add audit-v1.2 Prompt A: roles/teams loader recognition'); these are methodology prompts, not governance close-outs. Governance snapshot heads remained at SES-064 / DEC-204 / PI-045, so this conversation's claim on SES-065 / DEC-205 stands without rebase. The parallel-sandbox identifier-collision pattern named in the kickoff (three occurrences in the recent backfill program) did not trigger this time, though the watchpoint remains: PI-032 Code Change Lifecycle methodology rollout is the home for the reserve-at-apply-time identifier model that would eliminate this class of collisions.

(7) Sandbox commit and push. The five deliverable files are committed and pushed together in one commit per the sandbox push convention: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_065.json (this payload), PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-065.md (the apply prompt for Doug to run via Claude Code), and the three PI-045 implementation prompts at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-045-{A,B,C}-*.md.

(8) In-flight at end. The Claude Code coding conversation that runs PI-045 prompts A, B, C in sequence (this is the immediate next action after Doug applies SES-065 locally). After the code-changes conversation closes, the operational conversation follows: move crmbuilder.com nameservers from current registrar to Cloudflare (free plan, ~15 minutes); install cloudflared as a launchd service on Doug's laptop; create the named tunnel and DNS record routing mcp.crmbuilder.com to 127.0.0.1:8810; create the Cloudflare Access policy requiring Google login on mcp.crmbuilder.com; register the MCP URL in claude.ai's MCP integration with the X-CRMBuilder-Secret custom header. The smoke-test conversation that exercises all 44 MCP tools end-to-end against both CRMBUILDER and CBM engagements follows the operational conversation and produces the documentation deliverables (crmbuilder-v2/docs/remote-deployment.md and the crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md entry) as part of its close-out.

Real-time apply path consumed DEP-016 and DEP-017 between kickoff authoring and this conversation's open (SES-063 and SES-064 close-out applies). During this conversation's authoring window, PI-045's planning conversation closed and committed its SES-065 close-out payload to origin at 9a465a0 — that apply, when run, will consume DEP-018. This conversation's SES-066 close-out apply will consume DEP-019. Under the conversation's Option-A identifier allocation, PI-026's DEP range therefore becomes DEP-020 through DEP-045 pre-orphan-shrink and DEP-020 through DEP-043 after the orphan-CONV constraint shrink described below.

Decision 1 — references in wrote_record edges (DEC-206). The kickoff's literal scope language enumerated "session / decisions / planning_items / references" as the four target-type sets for wrote_record back-edges. Phase 1's backfill_governance_phase_1.py deliberately skipped references with the inline comment "skip references — REF-NNNN lookup adds complexity for limited value during backfill" and set records_summary.references=0 on DEP-001..008 to satisfy the spec's count-equals-edge-count cross-check. PI-026 settled this in favor of the kickoff's literal scope (Option 2): the backfill script's reference resolver maps each payload reference entry to its REF-NNNN identifier and authors a wrote_record edge per resolvable reference. Outcome differs from Phase 1's precedent by 125 reference edges (the resolvable subset of 139 payload references across the 24-record post-shrink inventory). Phase 1's 8 historical references remain orphan from the apply-event graph due to deposit_event immutability (records_summary cannot be amended via PATCH); documented in in_flight_at_end.

Decision 2 — identifier allocation (DEC-207). Kickoff-flagged surface-and-confirm question with kickoff-default Option A. Option A continues the sequential allocation pattern Phase 1 used (COP-001..008 are sequential, not session-matched); PI-026's COPs allocate COP-009..COP-032 and DEPs allocate DEP-020..DEP-043 in inventory order. Option B (match-to-SES for backfilled records) would have required either schema-level identifier-format change to admit a backfill suffix or an offset numbering range, plus retroactive renumbering of Phase 1's records (blocked by deposit_event immutability for the DEP side). Option A approved; the resulting convention reads "backfilled COPs/DEPs are sequential; real-time records match the session they apply to."

Decision 3 — reference resolution strategy (DEC-208). Three data-quality patterns surfaced during inventory authoring: (a) 132 payload references resolve directly via forward-direction match in the references snapshot; (b) 3 payload references in ses_001.json resolve only via reverse-direction match — the payload stores them as decision->session decided_in while the snapshot stores them as session->decision decided_in (REF-0001/0002/0003, the first three references ever created in the database); (c) 4 payload references in ses_030.json are unresolvable in either direction because DEC-105/106/107 are decided_in SES-036 in the snapshot rather than SES-030 (ses_030 and ses_036 share the same title and appear to be duplicate-session artifacts of an early re-application). Settled: backfill script's reference resolver tries forward match first, then reverse match, then skips with a logged data-quality exception. Skipped references reduce both the wrote_record edge count and the records_summary.references field for the affected DEP by the matching count.

Decision 4 — apply_context conventions (DEC-209). Kickoff-flagged surface-and-settle questions resolved per kickoff defaults. apply_script_version="backfill" (the actual version that ran the historical apply is not recorded; the runner field carries the provenance). invocation synthesized as "backfill_pi_026: ../PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_NNN.json" per close-out JSON. runner="backfill_script". log_file_path follows Phase 1's dep_NNN-historical.log pattern with three-line content noting the apply ran pre-v0.7 and was backfilled by scripts/backfill_pi_026_historical_applies_deposit_events.py on the apply date.

Decision 5 — 24-record scope (DEC-210). close_out_payload.md §3.4.5 requires every COP to have exactly one outbound close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation edge at all statuses, enforced at POST time with HTTP 422 payload_requires_producing_conversation_edge if the edge is absent. The kickoff's 26-JSON inventory included SES-001 and SES-046; neither session has a corresponding CONV record (both are in DEC-197's orphan-session list — SES-001..010 predate systematic workstream tracking; SES-046 is the WS-001-establishing conversation Phase 1 explicitly left orphan). PI-026 cannot author COP-records for those two sessions without first backfilling their CONV records, and CONV records themselves require workstream parents which are not yet established for the pre-workstream-era. Settled: defer SES-001 and SES-046 close-outs alongside DEC-197's existing orphan pattern. PI-026's scope shrinks from 26 to 24 close-outs; COP range becomes COP-009..COP-032 and DEP range becomes DEP-020..DEP-043; total wrote_record edges becomes 221 (24 sessions + 63 decisions + 9 planning items + 125 references).

Inventory deliverable approved. The 24-row per-COP allocation table mapping each historical close-out JSON to its allocated COP identifier, allocated DEP identifier, CONV parent identifier, and parsed record counts was presented as a block and approved before the artifact-authoring step. The data structure feeds directly into the backfill script's _CLOSE_OUTS list.

title: PI-029 slice B build planning — derived endpoint scope, PATCH updatability scope, by-sha endpoint specifics, list sort/order parameters; Claude Code prompt for commit access layer + REST endpoints + tests authored
topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Project default operating mode: ARCHITECTURE.\n\nUse PROTOTYPE mode when explicitly declared in a kickoff prompt for early exploratory work — first-cut schemas, draft methodologies, paste-and-iterate exploration where speed of testing matters more than completeness.\n\nUse DETAIL mode when explicitly declared for code writing in the crmbuilder repo, Claude Code prompt authoring, or surgical edits to existing files..." attached to the conversation's opening turn alongside the PI-029 slice B kickoff path (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-029-slice-b-commit-access-layer-and-rest-endpoints-kickoff.md).

Pre-flight verification. Pulled the crmbuilder repo at HEAD 8bf65b1 (the PI-029 slice B kickoff commit itself, with no intervening commits), read CLAUDE.md including the v0.7 governance entity release section and the working conventions push convention. Read commit.md v1.0 fully — §3.2 fields and validation, §3.3 relationships including the DEC-199 FK-over-references-edge deviation, §3.4 status-free documentary lifecycle with mis-ingestion correction posture and soft-delete semantics, §3.5 endpoint set with the new by-sha four-case behavior, §3.6 UI scope (PI-031, out of scope for slice B), §3.7 acceptance criteria 4-11 (slice B's coverage gate), §3.8 open questions for build-planning. Read slice A's commit 9c1d3b7 commit message to verify what landed (migration 0012, Commit ORM model lines 918-1002 in models.py, vocab.py v0.8 updates, 21 new tests, migration applied to CRMBUILDER engagement DB). Read the work_tickets repository pattern at access/repositories/work_tickets.py (447 lines) as the closest CRUD orchestration parallel, the work_tickets router at api/routers/work_tickets.py (122 lines), the _governance helpers at access/repositories/_governance.py (the shared field validators and edge helpers), the Pydantic schema patterns at api/schemas.py around the WorkTicketCreateIn cluster, and the work_ticket access-layer test patterns at tests/crmbuilder_v2/access/test_work_ticket.py. Confirmed the references API write contract uses the field key 'relationship' (per GovernanceEdgeIn schema and access/repositories/references.py line 68 to_dict rename of relationship_kind → relationship); the SES-066 apply prompt's spot-check curl snippet line 133 uses 'relationship_kind' but reads from the API response body where the key is 'relationship' — a latent typo in that snippet that does not block the apply, just produces a less-useful spot-check output. Not in scope to fix here.

Path correction surfaced. The kickoff cited two target paths: access/commit.py and api/commits.py. The repo convention verified by inspecting every existing governance entity module is access/repositories/<entity>.py (under repositories/, file name plural to match the table) and api/routers/<entity>.py (under routers/). Slice B prompt uses the correct paths regardless; the kickoff typo is noted in the prompt's Net Effect block so any future cross-reference is unambiguous.

Decision 1 — derived endpoint scope (DEC-211). The kickoff's Q1 named two candidate derived endpoints from commit.md §3.8.1: GET /conversations/{conversation_identifier}/commits (one-hop FK lookup) and GET /workstreams/{workstream_identifier}/commits (two-hop traversal through conversations). The eight-element decision template was applied — concrete example used SES-063 (the PI-028 commit-schema-spec conversation that produced the spec commit and the slice A merge commit 9c1d3b7), then the workstream-level example for the Code Change Lifecycle workstream that PI-029 establishes. Three options named with concrete behavior: A ship both, B ship only the one-hop, C ship neither. The recommendation was B with one-line rationale that frequency-of-traversal justifies the one-hop derived endpoint by the same logic DEC-199 used to justify the FK column, while the two-hop workstream endpoint is speculative without operational evidence — adding it now repeats the pattern DEC-133 was written to discourage. Honest costs of B documented: one extra route handler plus three tests, asymmetry across governance entities until or unless future build-planning conversations add parallels (work_tickets, close_out_payloads currently have no conversation-scoped derived endpoint), client-side fan-out cost for workstream-scoped queries until PI-032+ adds the two-hop endpoint. Approved. The derived endpoint is mounted on the conversations router rather than the commits router so the URL path nests naturally under /conversations.

Decision 2 — PATCH updatability scope (DEC-212). The kickoff's Q3 surfaced an ambiguity in commit.md §3.4.3 about whether commit_parent_shas is identity-like (in which case PATCH should reject changes) or correctable metadata (in which case PATCH should admit changes). The spec's §3.4.3 language admits PATCH for 'any correction that doesn't change the commit's identity' but does not explicitly enumerate which fields qualify as identity. Q4 was a parallel question about commit_files_changed_count. Settled: the identity-fields-only restriction stays on commit_identifier and commit_sha; every other field including commit_parent_shas, commit_files_changed_count, and commit_conversation_id is updatable via PATCH for the administrative-correction path. Rationale: the rare-correction case the spec's PATCH path was designed for is exactly the mis-ingestion scenario where the helper script (PI-030 work) captures the wrong parent SHAs from git log --format=%P or the wrong file-change count from git diff-tree, or the wrong conversation attribution per methodology §5.6's between-conversations-manual-commit concern. Narrowing PATCH to a smaller field set would force soft-delete-and-recreate for those scenarios, which §3.4.3 explicitly characterizes as 'reserved for the case where the correction changes the entity identity' — and parent SHAs, file counts, and conversation attribution are NOT identity. The DB-level constraint that does protect identity (UNIQUE on commit_sha) holds regardless.

Decision 3 — by-sha endpoint specifics (DEC-213). The kickoff's Q2 named three sub-questions: (a) minimum prefix length; (b) case normalization; (c) ambiguous-prefix 409 response body shape. Settled together as one decision since they are different facets of the same endpoint contract. (a) Minimum prefix length 4 hex characters per spec §3.5.1's '4+' language; prefixes of length 1-3 return HTTP 422 with code prefix_too_short. Git's --short default is 7 but the spec settled on 4 and the conversation did not surface evidence to bump it. (b) Uppercase or mixed-case input is lowercased before the query rather than rejected with HTTP 422; the schema stores lowercase per §3.2.1, and rejecting uppercase would be a worse API ergonomic than transparently normalizing. (c) Ambiguous-prefix 409 response body uses the field name 'candidates' (per the spec's sketch) carrying full 40-char SHAs only (not identifiers, not both); the caller already has SHAs as the natural key, so returning the V2 identifiers in the candidate list would invite confusion about which is canonical.

Decision 4 — list endpoint sort and order parameters (DEC-214). The kickoff's Q5 asked whether the list endpoint's default sort and the sort/order query parameter shape should match the V2 list-endpoint pattern. Settled: API default is commit_committed_at descending matching the UI master pane convention from commit.md §3.6.2 and parallel to deposit_event's deviation from V2's default ascending sort per the audit-log nature of the entity. The endpoint accepts ?sort=<column> and ?order=asc|desc per V2's list-endpoint pattern; the sortable column set is locked at the access layer to a known-safe subset (commit_identifier, commit_committed_at, commit_repository, commit_created_at, commit_updated_at) to prevent free-form column injection via the sort parameter. Invalid sort columns return HTTP 422 with code invalid_sort_column; invalid order values return HTTP 422 with code invalid_order.

Deliverable authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-029-B-commit-access-layer-and-rest.md — 2062-line Claude Code prompt with full code skeletons for the new repository module (commits.py), the new REST router (commits.py at api/routers/), the new test files (test_commit.py for access layer, test_commit_api.py for REST endpoints), the surgical edits to _helpers.py (next_prefixed_identifier gains a width keyword default 3 for backward compatibility), schemas.py (three new Pydantic classes), main.py (router registration), and conversations.py (the derived endpoint per DEC-211). The prompt's Pre-flight section includes a check that the running crmbuilder-v2-api process has been restarted to pick up the v0.8 vocab from slice A — slice A's commit message flagged that PID 3226223 (or whatever the current PID is) holds pre-v0.8 vocab in memory and must be restarted before tests can validate end-to-end. Test count target documented as 35+ new passing across the two new files, with the post-slice expectation of >= 1425 total v2 suite passing (up from slice A's 1391 baseline).

topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Open the PI-045 operational deployment conversation. Read the kickoff at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-045-remote-access-deployment-kickoff.md` and proceed with part (b)."

Structured summary of the operational conversation:

(1) Orientation and reframing. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md and the PI-045 kickoff. Verified status of PI-045's anchor decisions DEC-201..204 via the db-export decisions snapshot. Confirmed the planning conversation SES-065 had applied (DEC-205 fail-loud on marker drift; five decide-and-announce items settled — MCP HTTP port 8810, header X-CRMBuilder-Secret, secret storage at ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret with launchd-plist sourcing, Cloudflare Access Google login matching Doug's owner identity, documentation home at crmbuilder-v2/docs/remote-deployment.md). Confirmed PI-045 status was 'Open' — code-changes conversation (slices A/B/C) not yet applied. Surfaced the sequencing observation to Doug: operational steps 1–4 are infrastructure-independent of MCP code and can proceed without it being live; step 5 (claude.ai MCP integration registration) is gated on the HTTP transport actually being up. Doug approved 'proceed with steps 1–4, gate step 5.'

(2) Two clerical clarifications surfaced during orientation. The kickoff and SES-065 throughout name the domain as 'crmbuilder.com' but Doug's actual domain is 'crmbuilder.ai' (singular, no 's' — initial pasted-back 'crmbuilders.ai' was a typo that got reconciled to crmbuilder.ai across this session). The hostname under DEC-204 thus becomes 'mcp.crmbuilder.ai' instead of 'mcp.crmbuilder.com'. Clerical drift, no architectural change — DEC-204's substantive decision (Cloudflare Tunnel + Cloudflare Access identity gating on a single subdomain under Doug's zone) is preserved unchanged. Separately, the kickoff and SES-065 specified launchd-plist for the service manager assuming macOS, but Doug's machine is Linux Mint 22 — substituted systemd user units throughout. Both clarifications are captured in this session's narrative rather than as supersedes edges since they don't change any settled architectural decision.

(3) Step 1 — nameserver migration crmbuilder.ai GoDaddy → Cloudflare. Cloudflare zone added at dash.cloudflare.com with team name 'crmbuilder' (replacing the auto-assigned 'summer-scene-4e25'), team_domain crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com, free plan. Cloudflare's DNS scan auto-imported 13 records; reviewed each row against the GoDaddy ground-truth panel. Deleted 4 GoDaddy-default records (2 A records to GoDaddy parking IPs 15.197.148.33 and 3.33.130.190; the cname _domainconnect → _domainconnect.gd.domaincontrol.com which is GoDaddy's auto-DNS handshake protocol; the cname www → crmbuilder.ai which pointed to the parking apex). Kept all 10 substantive records (5 Google Workspace MX records aspmx.l.google.com and alt1–4 with correct priorities; TXT google-site-verification for Google Workspace ownership; apex TXT v=spf1 include:dc-aa8e722993._spfm.crmbuilder.ai ~all; TXT google._domainkey for DKIM signature; TXT _dmarc v=DMARC1 p=quarantine). Cloudflare's scanner missed the delegated SPF subdomain TXT at dc-aa8e722993._spfm — added manually via the Add Record dialog with type=TXT, name=dc-aa8e722993._spfm, content=v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all (without this record, outbound Google Workspace mail would have started failing SPF validation as a void lookup after nameserver flip). At GoDaddy, switched custom nameservers to grant.ns.cloudflare.com and luciane.ns.cloudflare.com. Cloudflare dashboard initially showed 'Invalid nameservers' (timing/staleness; whois confirmed both Cloudflare nameservers at the .ai registry; resolved within ~30 minutes when Cloudflare's recheck ran). Verified email records survived migration with dig commands at the local resolver after Cloudflare reported zone Active: NS resolves to both Cloudflare nameservers, all 5 MX records present, apex SPF + Google-site-verification TXTs present, the manually-added dc-aa8e722993._spfm TXT resolves correctly, google._domainkey DKIM resolves with the full RSA public key (two-string split per DNS protocol for keys >255 chars), _dmarc TXT resolves with the v=DMARC1 policy intact. Step 1 closed.

(4) Step 2 — install cloudflared and authenticate. Initial apt install via the pkg.cloudflare.com repo failed because lsb_release -cs returned 'zara' (the Linux Mint 22 codename) and Cloudflare's apt repo is keyed on upstream Ubuntu codenames — overwrote /etc/apt/sources.list.d/cloudflared.list to hardcode 'noble' (Mint 22's Ubuntu base) and reran apt update + install. cloudflared 2026.5.0 installed cleanly at /usr/local/bin/cloudflared (and also /usr/bin/cloudflared; both paths point to identical binaries from the same deb install). Pre-existing VirtualBox 7.0.16 DKMS failure against kernel 6.17 surfaced in the apt output as 'dpkg returned an error code (1)' but is unrelated to cloudflared and was already pending from prior apt state — flagged for separate cleanup, out of PI-045 scope. cloudflared tunnel login opened a browser to Cloudflare, Doug selected the crmbuilder.ai zone and authorized, the zone certificate was saved to ~/.cloudflared/cert.pem (mode 0600, ~5–10 KB). Step 2 closed.

(5) Step 3 — tunnel + DNS + systemd user service. Created the named tunnel 'crmbuilder-mcp' (UUID 6170403a-eb17-47ee-af94-ea70de6a1e74) via cloudflared tunnel create; tunnel credentials written to ~/.cloudflared/6170403a-eb17-47ee-af94-ea70de6a1e74.json. Created the public DNS route via cloudflared tunnel route dns crmbuilder-mcp mcp.crmbuilder.ai — Cloudflare added the CNAME mcp.crmbuilder.ai → <UUID>.cfargotunnel.com. Wrote ~/.cloudflared/config.yml with tunnel + credentials-file + ingress rule (hostname mcp.crmbuilder.ai → service http://127.0.0.1:8810) and the required terminal http_status:404 catch-all. Foreground test (cloudflared tunnel run crmbuilder-mcp) registered four Cloudflare edge connections; external curl https://mcp.crmbuilder.ai through cloudflared's edge cert (Let's Encrypt wildcard *.crmbuilder.ai, the cert that Cloudflare provisioned automatically on the zone) returned HTTP 502 via 172.67.219.60 as expected (tunnel routes correctly, no origin listening on :8810). Wrapped under systemd user service at ~/.config/systemd/user/crmbuilder-mcp-tunnel.service with ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/cloudflared --no-autoupdate --config /home/doug/.cloudflared/config.yml tunnel run crmbuilder-mcp, Restart=on-failure, RestartSec=5s, WantedBy=default.target. sudo loginctl enable-linger doug enables the service to keep running without an active login session. systemctl --user daemon-reload + enable + start brought the service to 'Active: active (running)', and a re-run of the curl test under systemd-managed cloudflared returned the same HTTP 502 via Cloudflare edge IP. Two log warnings noted as non-blocking and unrelated to HTTP tunneling: ICMP proxy disabled because GID 1000 is not in ping_group_range (cloudflared can optionally proxy ICMP through tunnels but this feature is unused for HTTP-only routing); QUIC receive buffer 'failed to sufficiently increase' from kernel default 208 KiB to cloudflared's preferred 7168 KiB (got 416 KiB; affects throughput on high-volume QUIC flows, not low-volume MCP traffic; tunable via net.core.rmem_max if it ever matters). Step 3 closed.

(6) Step 4 — Cloudflare Access setup; two consequential decisions surfaced inline, captured as DEC-225. First surfaced decision (option A vs B vs C in the 'Surface and settle' on Cloudflare Access for programmatic clients): SES-065's settled 'Google login (Doug's identity)' assumed a browser-only client and would 302-redirect claude.ai's MCP integration on every request (claude.ai cannot follow OAuth redirects). Three options framed — single Access app with two Include rules under one Allow policy; two Access applications under different hostnames; or drop Cloudflare Access entirely and rely solely on the shared-secret middleware. Recommended Option A (preserves DEC-204's dual-layer security model with minimum complexity); Doug approved 'a'. Second surfaced decision: in the middle of configuring the Google IdP, Cloudflare's UI required an App ID — Cloudflare has retired the built-in Google OAuth shortcut and now requires bring-your-own Google Cloud OAuth credentials for the Google IdP. The 'use Cloudflare's built-in Google OAuth credentials' half of SES-065's decide-and-announce on Google login was thus no longer viable as written. Three replacement options framed — set up Doug's own Google OAuth (Google Cloud project, consent screen, credentials, ~15 min upfront and ongoing project maintenance); use Cloudflare's already-enabled One-time PIN identity provider (no setup, per-login PIN-check friction); or any other federated IdP (similar third-party setup overhead to Google). Recommended Option B (One-time PIN for low-frequency single-user admin path; zero setup beats ongoing Google Cloud project management; claude.ai's path is the actually-important one and is handled by the Service Token from Option A above). Doug approved 'approve'. Both auth-model revisions consolidated into DEC-225 with a references edge to DEC-204.

(7) Step 4 implementation. Substantial Cloudflare-One UI navigation friction was encountered because Cloudflare reorganized Zero Trust into Cloudflare One since the kickoff was authored: the old 'Settings → Authentication → Login methods' path no longer exists; identity providers now live at 'Integrations → Identity providers' (the renamed Login Methods page) and service tokens at 'Access controls → Service credentials → Service Tokens'. Created service token 'claude-ai-mcp' with non-expiring duration; captured Client ID + Client Secret from the one-time modal. Initial credential-file write polluted both ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-id and ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-secret with the header-name prefix from copy-paste ('CF-Access-Client-Id: 39ac09…' instead of just '39ac09…'); the doubled header-name caused early verbose-curl-grep output to show '> CF-Access-Client-Id: CF-Access-Client-Id: 39ac09d4d7c707c75c2b9187a896d67c.access'. Stripped the prefix in place using tail -c +22 / +26 to avoid re-pasting (which would have risked re-introducing the same error). Result: ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-id = 39 bytes ending in '.access', cf-access-client-secret = 64 hex bytes, both mode 0600. Hygiene followup: the Client Secret value appeared in conversation transcript during the diagnostic; blast radius is currently zero (no MCP backend listening, no shared-secret middleware yet), but rotation is required before step 5 / claude.ai integration goes live — captured in in_flight_at_end.

(8) Created Self-hosted Access Application 'CRM Builder MCP' protecting subdomain=mcp, domain=crmbuilder.ai, path=blank (matches all paths). Application uses One-time PIN as identity provider with 'Accept all available identity providers' toggle on (effectively OTP-only since OTP is the only enabled IdP). Created two Access policies. Policy 'Allow admin' — Action=Allow, Include rule Selector=Emails Value=admin@crmbuilder.ai, session duration 24 hours. Policy 'Service Auth claude-ai-mcp' — Action=Service Auth, Include rule Selector=Service Token Value=claude-ai-mcp. Both attached to the CRM Builder MCP application via the application's Access policies section. The two-policy split is essential — initial single-policy attempt with both Include rules under Allow returned HTTP 302 on service-token requests because Cloudflare honors service-token validation only under Action=Service Auth (or via a separate Service Auth policy attached to the same app), not as an Include rule under Allow. Diagnostic chain: verbose curl confirmed the CF-Access-Client-Id and CF-Access-Client-Secret headers were being sent correctly; the JWT meta in the 302's Location header showed 'service_token_status: false, auth_status: NONE' which means Cloudflare's edge was not validating the token; root cause traced to the Service Auth policy existing in the global Policies library but never having been attached to the application (Cloudflare's app-creation wizard creates an inline policy but does not auto-attach external policies created later — they must be added via the application's 'Add existing policy' control). After explicit attachment, the application's Access policies section showed both 'Allow admin' (Action=ALLOW) and 'Service Auth claude-ai-mcp' (Action=SERVICE AUTH); service-token requests began returning HTTP 502 from the tunnel as expected.

(9) Three auth paths verified end-to-end. Path 1 (no auth): HTTP 302 to https://crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com/cdn-cgi/access/login/mcp.crmbuilder.ai with JWT meta showing auth_status=NONE; confirms Access policy is enforced. Path 2 (Service Token): HTTP 502 with curl bearing CF-Access-Client-Id and CF-Access-Client-Secret headers from the credential files; the 502 is from the origin layer through the tunnel since 127.0.0.1:8810 is not listening yet; confirms claude.ai's path will work once the MCP HTTP transport from PI-045 slice A is up. Path 3 (browser + OTP): visited https://mcp.crmbuilder.ai in a browser, entered admin@crmbuilder.ai at the Cloudflare Access login page, received the 6-digit One-time PIN by email, pasted into the browser, was redirected to the origin and saw the Cloudflare 502 error page; confirms browser admin path is functional. Step 4 closed. PI-045 operational steps 1–4 complete.

(10) Identifier rebase. At conversation start the heads were SES-068 / DEC-215 / PI-047. By close-out time, three parallel-sandbox commits had landed: 1435f0a 'Apply SES-069 close-out: PI-023 reconciliation utility planning — DEC-216..220 and PI-048', 232e967 'PI-030 architecture planning close-out (SES-070) — payload, apply prompt, and three slice prompts' (payload file present, apply not yet run), and b34391f 'PI-022 governance-backfill program closed — PI-022 status Open -> Resolved'. Heads in the snapshot at close-out time: SES-069, DEC-220, PI-048 in the applied set; SES-070, DEC-221–224 claimed in the unapplied repo file. This conversation rebased its anticipated SES-069 / DEC-216 to SES-071 / DEC-225 — first rebase to skip an unapplied-but-claimed identifier (SES-070), fifth occurrence of the parallel-sandbox pattern in the recent program. The apply prompt for this conversation accommodates two head states pre-apply (either SES-069/DEC-220 if SES-070 has not yet been applied, or SES-070/DEC-224 if it has).

(11) Two files committed and pushed together at close-out per sandbox convention: this payload at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_071.json and the apply prompt at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-071.md.
