title: PI-045 code-changes implementation — slices A (transport flag + FastMCP HTTP binding), B (X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware), C (engagement-marker fail-loud guard per DEC-205) executed in sequence at Doug's terminal via Claude Code; MCP HTTP transport now live on 127.0.0.1:8810 with two-layer authentication (Cloudflare Access edge + shared-secret app middleware) and DEC-205's drift guard on the REST API; PI-045 code-changes scope structurally complete
topics_covered:
Seed prompts: each slice executed against its dedicated `CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-045-{A,B,C}-*.md` prompt authored in SES-065. Slice A: 'PI-045 slice A — `--transport` flag and FastMCP HTTP binding' (operating mode DETAIL, predecessor none, successor slice B). Slice B: '...slice B — shared-secret middleware'. Slice C: '...slice C — engagement-marker fail-loud guard per DEC-205'.

Structured summary of the code-changes conversation across all three slices:

(1) Slice A — transport flag and FastMCP HTTP binding. Added `mcp_http_port: int = 8810` to `crmbuilder_v2/config.py`'s Settings class, env-var-driven via `CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_HTTP_PORT`, matching the existing `api_host` / `api_port` env-var pattern. Host hardcoded to `127.0.0.1` per DEC-202 — not exposed as a setting since making it user-configurable would invite a future foot-gun where it could be set to `0.0.0.0` and bypass DEC-202's MCP-only public exposure intent. Added `--transport {stdio,streamable-http}` and `--port <int>` argparse flags to the `crmbuilder-v2-mcp` CLI entry point in `crmbuilder_v2/cli.py`; defaults preserve existing desktop-piped behavior. Parameterized `mcp_server/server.py` `main()` to dispatch on transport — the call site that previously read `server.run('stdio')` at line 44 now reads `server.run(transport=transport, host=host, port=port)`. Tests in `tests/test_transport_dispatch.py` cover argparse choices (stdio default, streamable-http override, --port int conversion) and the build-and-dispatch path (verifies FastMCP.run is called with the right kwargs for each transport). No authentication in this slice — the HTTP transport accepts any unauthenticated request, useful only for local end-to-end smoke testing against the laptop until slice B lands.

(2) Slice B — shared-secret header middleware. Added `mcp_shared_secret: str | None = None` to Settings, env-var-driven via `CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET`. Startup hard-fails (RuntimeError with the explicit message 'CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET must be set when transport is streamable-http (DEC-204 second-layer auth)' and exit code 1) if streamable-http is selected without the secret being set — preserving DEC-204's two-layer authentication intent and preventing the server from accidentally going live without app-layer auth. New module `crmbuilder_v2/mcp_server/middleware.py` exposes `SharedSecretMiddleware` (Starlette-compatible) that validates the `X-CRMBuilder-Secret` request header against `Settings.mcp_shared_secret` via `hmac.compare_digest` (constant-time comparison; resistant to timing-oracle attacks). On missing or wrong header, the middleware returns HTTP 401 with JSON body `{"error":"unauthorized"}` before route dispatch; on match, the request passes through to FastMCP's routing. The middleware is registered on the streamable-http transport's underlying ASGI app; stdio transport bypasses the middleware entirely (no header to validate when the transport is a process pipe). Verification matrix run end-to-end: (i) `unset CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET; crmbuilder-v2-mcp --transport streamable-http` → RuntimeError with the named message, exit code 1; (ii) `curl http://127.0.0.1:8813/` (no header) → HTTP 401 with `{"error":"unauthorized"}` and server log line 'rejected request GET / — missing or wrong X-CRMBuilder-Secret header'; (iii) `curl -H 'X-CRMBuilder-Secret: wrong' http://127.0.0.1:8813/` → same 401 + log; (iv) `curl -H 'X-CRMBuilder-Secret: $CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET' -H 'Accept: application/json, text/event-stream' http://127.0.0.1:8813/` → HTTP 404 ('Not Found') from FastMCP's routing — proves the middleware allowed the authenticated request through and the 401 vs 404 distinction is correctly middleware-vs-FastMCP-routing rather than a false positive. Slice B added 8 tests across 2 files (4 in `test_shared_secret_middleware.py`, 4 in `test_transport_dispatch.py` extending slice A's dispatch tests with auth-aware behavior). All 8 collected and passed in isolation.

(3) Slice C — engagement-marker fail-loud guard. Implements DEC-205's per-request fail-loud option (chosen in SES-065 over silent-follow after the planning conversation's codebase finding that `route_settings_to_engagement` is not a stat-and-read but a process-global state mutation incompatible with per-request rerouting). New module `crmbuilder_v2/api/marker_guard.py` with module-level `_MARKER_AT_START` capturing the engagement code that the API process bound to at startup, plus `EngagementMarkerGuardMiddleware` (Starlette-compatible) that on every non-exempt request stats the marker file `data/current_engagement.json`, reads the `engagement_code` field, compares to `_MARKER_AT_START`, and returns HTTP 409 with the structured body `{"error":"engagement_marker_changed","marker_at_start":"<captured>","marker_now":"<live>","action":"restart API to bind to new engagement"}` plus a WARNING log line `marker_guard: request <METHOD> <PATH> rejected — marker_at_start='<captured>' marker_now='<live>'` on mismatch. `cli.run_api()` calls `set_marker_at_start(active_code)` after the existing engagement resolution and before `uvicorn.run`, capturing the resolved active_code (which may have come from the `--engagement` CLI flag override or the marker file) — not the live marker; processes started with `--engagement` override stay bound to that engagement regardless of later marker changes. Exempt paths `/health`, `/openapi.json`, `/docs`, `/redoc` bypass the guard so liveness probes, OpenAPI consumers, and Swagger/ReDoc UIs continue to work mid-drift. Slice C's follow-up commit c578503b added a module-level `_UNINITIALIZED` sentinel object distinguishing 'marker not yet captured' from 'captured-as-None' (the latter being a legitimate state if the active_code happened to be None at startup — which shouldn't happen in practice but the type system can't rule out); the middleware's startup check now compares against the sentinel rather than `None`, and a `reset_marker_at_start_for_tests()` helper enables clean state reset across test cases. Slice C's second follow-up commit a9cfe138 tuned `api/main.py` middleware registration (specifically the middleware ordering within FastAPI's app initialization). Verification was end-to-end against the live API on port 8765: (i) happy path — marker says CRMBUILDER, `curl http://127.0.0.1:8765/sessions?limit=1` returns HTTP 200 with the CRMBUILDER engagement's most recent session SES-071 (PI-045 operational steps 1–4); (ii) drift path — while API still running, flipped marker to CBM via direct edit of `current_engagement.json`, then `curl http://127.0.0.1:8765/sessions?limit=1` returned HTTP 409 with the four expected body fields (error name, marker_at_start='CRMBUILDER', marker_now='CBM', action='restart API to bind to new engagement') and the WARNING log line naming both markers; (iii) health bypass — same drift state, `curl http://127.0.0.1:8765/health` returns HTTP 200 with `{"data":{"ok":true},"meta":{},"errors":null}` confirming the exempt-path bypass; (iv) restart fix — `kill $API_PID; wait; crmbuilder-v2-api &` (marker still CBM), then `curl http://127.0.0.1:8765/sessions?limit=1` returns HTTP 200 with CBM's SES-001 ('Methodology schemas — CBM content paper-test'), entirely different content from CRMBUILDER's SES-071, proving the restart re-bound the API process to CBM. Marker restored to CRMBUILDER (ENG-001) at the end. Slice C added 5 tests in `test_marker_guard_middleware.py` (4 from ea04e1a6 covering match/mismatch/missing-marker/exempt-bypass plus 1 `test_uninitialized_guard_bypasses` from c578503b's sentinel work). All 5 collected and passed in isolation. Full v2 collect-only count at HEAD ae7a77ea: 1489 tests.

(4) Three two-headed observations spanning the slices. First: the working tree picked up unrelated commits during the conversation (audit-v1.2 Prompts F and G; PI-030 slice A) — these landed on main since slice C started but did not collide with PI-045's identifier space or code surface; they're noted only because they appear in the linear history between this conversation's slice-B HEAD (209a3dc9) and slice-C HEAD (ea04e1a6). Second: the slice C prompt's Done block asked for a 'pointer that PI-045's code-changes scope is now complete' and named the path under `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/` where the operational step-5 conversation's kickoff prompt 'should land' — but slice C did not itself author that kickoff. The step-5 kickoff is therefore still to-be-authored (captured in in_flight_at_end). Third: this combined close-out is authored from the operational-conversation sandbox (SES-071's home) rather than from a fresh code-changes sandbox; option 1 per the SES-072 authoring agreement during the operational-conversation thread. This bundling treats the three Claude Code coding sessions as a single conceptual code-changes conversation with one governance record at the end — symmetric with how SES-071 captured PI-045 operational steps 1–4 as a single record and matches the kickoff's framing of 'the code-changes implementation conversation' as a single conversation.

(5) Identifier rebase. At authoring time the v2 governance heads were SES-071 / DEC-225 / PI-048 / COP-071 / DEP-050 / references=851. This close-out claims SES-072. No new decisions are surfaced (everything implemented in this conversation was downstream of pre-settled decisions); no new planning items are surfaced. The single payload reference is `session:SES-072 -[is_about]-> planning_item:PI-045`. Standard sandbox commit-and-push: payload + apply prompt committed and pushed together as one commit.

summary: PI-045 step 5 reached the claude.ai connector registration step and discovered that claude.ai's UI supports only OAuth 2.1 auth — no custom HTTP headers — making the X-CRMBuilder-Secret + Cloudflare Service Token model from DEC-204/225 incompatible. The rotation + mcp-secret persistence work completed cleanly (and end-to-end verified through the tunnel), but registration is blocked. DEC-226 settles the architectural reroute to OAuth 2.1 + PKCE; PI-049 captures the implementation scope with a timeboxed Path B (Cloudflare Access as OIDC IdP) feasibility check before committing to Path A (OAuth on our server). PI-045 is blocked by PI-049. Lesson: verify the integrating client's UI auth model before designing server-side auth that must plug into it.
title: PI-045 step-5 conversation: Service Token rotation + mcp-secret persistence completed, but claude.ai connector registration found to be incompatible with header-based auth; DEC-226 reroutes to OAuth 2.1 + PI-049 created to enable claude.ai integration
topics_covered: Service Token rotation (claude-ai-mcp Client ID rotated to 39ac09d4d7c707c75c2b9187a896d67c.access; Client Secret rotated; files at ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-{id,secret} updated; password manager updated; end-to-end Cloudflare Access verification curl returned HTTP 502 confirming the new token is accepted at the edge and traffic routed to the tunnel target). mcp-secret persistence (discovered missing — SES-072's setup had only set the value as an ephemeral env var; new 64-char hex secret generated via secrets.token_hex(32) and written to ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret mode 0600, also saved to password manager). MCP HTTP transport startup (uv run crmbuilder-v2-mcp --transport streamable-http with CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET sourced from the new file; Uvicorn confirmed listening on 127.0.0.1:8810; SharedSecretMiddleware initialized cleanly). End-to-end auth verification through the tunnel (curl with all three custom headers returned HTTP 404 from the MCP transport — Cloudflare Access accepted the Service Token (CF_Authorization JWT issued with common_name = 39ac09d4d7c707c75c2b9187a896d67c.access matching the new Client ID), tunnel routed, SharedSecretMiddleware accepted X-CRMBuilder-Secret, MCP transport answered with 404 because GET / isn't a route it serves — all four layers cooperated). claude.ai connector registration (navigated to Settings → Connectors → + Add connector → Add custom connector form with Name + Remote MCP server URL + Advanced settings collapsed; Advanced settings exposed only OAuth Client ID + OAuth Client Secret — no custom header configuration; alternative Browse Connector option is the marketplace, not self-hosted). Research into claude.ai's connector auth model (WebFetch of Anthropic support docs + WebSearch confirming claude.ai's connectors use OAuth 2.1 + PKCE per MCP spec; GitHub issue #112 'Cannot configure Authorization: Bearer for custom remote MCP' confirms custom HTTP headers are an open feature request not supported today). Three-path analysis (Path A: implement OAuth on our server, multi-slice work; Path B: use Cloudflare Access as OAuth IdP, less work if feasible; Path C: defer claude.ai integration, rejected as impractical because cut/paste friction is what PI-045 was created to eliminate). DEC-226 (replace X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware with OAuth 2.1; timeboxed Path B feasibility check first). PI-049 created (the OAuth implementation workstream). Direct-API writes via inline Python script of DEC-226 / PI-049 / SES-073 / WT-046 / CONV-046 plus wiring references — dogfooding the v0.7 governance entity model for session-to-session handoff (kickoff body stored in WT-046 rather than in a markdown file).

topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "PI-030 build closure — first nine-section close-out conversation — kickoff". Six surface-and-settle questions worked through in sequence as consequential decisions:

(1) Build-closure conversation-type pattern (DEC-232): Approved option A — a dedicated Claude.ai conversation owns the close-out for build work executed across multiple Claude Code sessions whose own surfaces don't author governance records. The build closure ingests executor commits via the commits section, resolves the upstream planning item, and surfaces any methodology drift the executors discovered. Alternatives B (planner defers its own close), C (per-Claude-Code-session build receipts), D (next planning conversation absorbs the prior build) rejected on closing-too-late, noise-multiplication, and false-attribution grounds respectively. Methodology §10 (new section) authored in this conversation establishes the pattern.

(2) Commit-array scoping under the bootstrap commits.json case + parallel-workstream interleaving (DEC-233): Approved option E — closure curates the three slice SHAs manually rather than invoking enumerate_commits.py. Discovered during pre-work that between slice-A's parent and current HEAD, the main-branch log contains 8 commits NOT owned by PI-030 (audit-v1.2 Prompts G/H/I, PI-045 code-changes apply + step-5 kickoff). The helper's since..HEAD range model assumes a single active workstream per repo; parallel workstreams break it. Option G (--since-sha flag from the kickoff's default) would over-ingest. Helper evolution surfaced as PI-050 (status Open) for future implementation; this conversation's commits array carries exactly three explicit entries per §4.1 shape pulled from git log.

(3) Resolves attribution scope (DEC-234): Approved option I — resolve PI-030 only in this closure; PI-027 (methodology drafting), PI-028 (commit schema spec), PI-029 (commits access layer + REST), and PI-032 (rollout) stay Open for PI-033's retroactive resolution per the existing methodology §9 posture. Pushed back on the kickoff's default J — resolving PI-027/028/029 here would point resolves edges from CONV-046 (this build closure) at planning items whose work was authored by prior conversations the closure didn't conduct, breaking the audit query in §6.1. The §9 retroactive-resolution discipline is an attribution rule, not a mechanism-availability stopgap.

(4) Executor-improvisation governance posture (DEC-235): Approved option N — no governance record for slice B's surgical reorder of conversations.py (identifier-conflict check before title-conflict check). Slice B's commit message documents the rationale verbatim and the test suite (TestPI030NewSections, 12 new tests including full-payload idempotency on re-run) captures the behavior. Pushed back on the kickoff's default L (Resolved-on-arrival PI) on attribution and methodology-creep grounds: the conversation that did the fix was slice B's executor session, not this closure; manufacturing a PI for every executor improvisation is methodology creep.

(5) Build-closure conversation_purpose convention (DEC-236): Decided as a follow-on to Q1 — option O — purpose statement opens with "Build closure for SES-NNN — " naming the upstream planner. Grep-discoverable convention; one-line addition to methodology §10. Routine choice; not surfaced through full eight-element template.

(6) Workstream membership for CONV-046 + methodology §4.0 example fix (DEC-237): Surfaced during pre-work by Claude after discovering that the conversations.py access layer requires every live conversation to carry exactly one conversation_belongs_to_workstream edge, and none of the eight existing workstreams (WS-001..WS-008, all status complete) covers PI-030's lineage. Approved option α — apply prompt POSTs /workstreams to create WS-009 "Code Change Lifecycle workstream" status in_flight as a pre-step before invoking apply_close_out.py; CONV-046's conversation block embeds the workstream-membership edge to WS-009 alongside the conversation_records_session edge to SES-074. Methodology §4.0's worked example gets a fix in passing — slice A's example showed only the session edge, a latent gap the access layer enforces. Historical conversations from SES-057 (Code Change Lifecycle workstream established), SES-061 (methodology drafted), SES-063 (commit schema spec), SES-067 (PI-029 slice B planning), SES-068 (PI-026 supersession close-out), SES-070 (PI-030 architecture planning) get retroactively assigned to WS-009 by PI-033's Phase 3 backfill.

Pre-work findings: all four expected SHAs present on origin/main (70d88e6 slice A, 2b5557d slice B, c6ff67a slice C, 232e967 SES-070 close-out apply); identifier heads SES-072 / DEC-225 / PI-048 / CONV-045 / COP-072 / DEP-051 captured from db-export snapshots; commits.json snapshot absent (bootstrap case for Q2); methodology §4.0 conversation-block amendment confirmed landed per slice A; PI-027/028/029/030/032 all confirmed Open.

topics_covered: Re-ran the pi-cleanup audit after Doug correctly flagged that the first version's keyword-scan against PI descriptions was structurally wrong: PI descriptions are immutable scope statements written at PI-creation time, not status fields, so they never get updated when work lands. The corrected method extracts the concrete artifacts each PI's description specifies (ORM models, alembic migrations, vocab entries, methodology document sections, snapshot record counts, file existence) and checks them against current codebase / snapshot state. Five parallel general-purpose subagents verified 36 of the 37 Open PIs across V2 schema/access/API, V1 deploy engine, methodology + governance content, V2 desktop UI panels, and governance backfill + deployment + auth scopes; the 37th (PI-001) was verified inline. Revised tally: 4 RESOLVE / 6 NEEDS-INPUT / 27 KEEP. Four RESOLVEs were missed-flip cases — substantive work landed in prior workstreams but no resolves edge was authored and no status update happened. PI-001 (full styling design pass per DEC-024): six v0.6 build slices A-F shipped across SES-037..SES-043 on 2026-05-18; status v16 payload narrates 'PI-001 is discharged' verbatim but the planning_item record was never flipped. PI-024 (PI-022 phase 2 — backfill prior workstreams): workstreams.json has 9 records covering the 7 prior workstreams the description lists, plus WS-001 (the v0.7 release itself) and WS-009 (Code Change Lifecycle); apply commit 7eec15d2 landed the records before the PI-022 umbrella closed. PI-025 (phase 3 — backfill prior conversations): conversations.json has 46 CONV records, all status 'complete', matching the orchestrator's target (37 + 8 Phase 1 + CONV-046 ad-hoc) after DEC-197 descoped 16 orphan sessions; apply commit af11e649 landed them. PI-026 (phase 4 — backfill historical applies as deposit_events): deposit_events.json has 32 historical-tagged records (DEP-001..008 from Phase 1 + DEP-020..043 from Phase 4), matching the DEC-210 24-payload scope tightening exactly; apply commit 944495e1 landed them with matching dep_NNN-historical.log placeholders. The PI-022 umbrella close commit b34391ff incorrectly characterized 'Phase 2 deferred' even though the backfill scripts had been applied days prior — a documented integrity drift this cleanup repairs. The six NEEDS-INPUT items (PI-023, PI-027, PI-028, PI-029, PI-032, PI-047) were deliberately not resolved in this pass: each requires a judgment call (stay-Open contracts for PI-027 / PI-028 pending PI-033 backfill; the missing StorageClient methods on PI-029; the unmet root CLAUDE.md cross-reference on PI-032; the file-vs-DB divergence on PI-047; the design redesign on PI-023). The 27 KEEPs all verified as positively not done — concrete artifact missing in each case (no ORM column, no vocab entry, no methodology section, no UI panel, no migration). Pattern surfaced by the corrected audit: the PI-022 backfill program's individual phases finished before the umbrella closed, so the umbrella-level close commit lost track of its own children's state. Same shape as PI-001: substantive work ships, governance bookkeeping misses the final flip.

summary: PI-049 Phase 1 Path B feasibility check completed end-to-end and revealed an upstream Anthropic bug in claude.ai's connector OAuth implementation — the bug is between receiving the authorization code and exchanging it for a token, reproducible across 7+ IdPs (Cloudflare being the 7th). Path B infrastructure preserved as-configured on disk and recoverable. Strategic pivot to chat-UI-on-Anthropic-API with native tool definitions calling the REST API directly removes the third-party dependency on claude.ai-web. The original goal (a chat UI with v2 governance tools) is achievable today via a simpler architecture; the months of foundational v2 work (structured DB, REST API, MCP tools, methodology) is unaffected and load-bearing for the new path. Only PI-045 (remote MCP) and PI-049 (OAuth implementation) are shelved-blocked pending Anthropic fix.
title: v2 AI surface integration — claude.ai-web blocked by upstream Anthropic connector bug (ofid_* pattern across 7+ IdPs), chat-UI-on-Anthropic-API pivot; Path B fully configured + shelved with infrastructure preserved
topics_covered: SES-074 apply (misapply diagnosis: prompt typo on the payload filename caused ses_073.json no-op-application before ses_074.json was applied correctly; lazy DEP-053/054 + prompt file path fix). PI-049 Phase 1 (Path B feasibility): research on Cloudflare Access SaaS-OIDC vs. Managed OAuth, MCP authorization spec, claude.ai connector OAuth model. Discovery that Cloudflare Access has FIRST-CLASS MCP authorization support via the Managed OAuth feature on self-hosted Access apps — serves spec-compliant Protected Resource Metadata at /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource, team-domain Authorization Server Metadata at /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server with code_challenge_methods_supported: [S256], DCR endpoint, refresh tokens, RFC 8707 audience binding. Dashboard configuration: a SaaS-OIDC application was created and then deleted (wrong pattern); the existing self-hosted CRM Builder MCP Access app already had the Managed OAuth feature with claude.ai/claude.com redirect URIs registered. claude.ai connector registration end-to-end test: OAuth dance succeeded through One-time PIN authentication and user consent screen; then failed with `ofid_64b9bbb2f1378a53` error. MCP server log /tmp/mcp.log confirmed no post-OAuth requests reached the server. Diagnosis: Anthropic GitHub issue #271 + 6 siblings document the same silent failure pattern across WorkOS, Clerk, Salesforce, M365 IdPs — the bug is in claude.ai's connector code path between receiving the authorization code and exchanging it for a token, deterministic, silent, no Anthropic resolution. Strategic reframing: Doug clarified the original goal was a chat UI with v2 governance tools accessible, not specifically claude.ai-web as the surface. Discussion of alternatives: (a) mcp-connector via Anthropic API — programmatic, doesn't help with claude.ai-web UI, helps with building agents/scripts; (b) oauth_anthropic_creds — third claude.ai registration approach where Anthropic holds creds, requires manual review email to mcp-review@anthropic.com, may bypass the bug, unknown timeline; (c) Claude Code / Claude Desktop with stdio MCP — fully working today, what Doug has been using throughout this conversation; (d) custom chat UI on Anthropic API with native tool definitions calling REST API directly — bypasses MCP entirely, smaller and cleaner than any MCP-based remote access. Doug asked sharp clarifying question: why bother with MCP at all if writing the chat UI in Python that talks to the REST API directly? Answer: for one client, in the same language as the server, talking to one backend on localhost, MCP is pure ceremony. The MCP server's tools.py module is reusable as native tool definitions for the Anthropic API; the structured DB, REST API, PySide6 desktop app, methodology work, MCP stdio mode for Claude Desktop are all unaffected. Code disposition: option A (commit-as-shelved single commit, fc4690c) preserves the Path B work — middleware.py deleted, server.py with streamable_http_path='/' added, config.py without mcp_shared_secret — recoverable when/if Anthropic ships connector fix. Documentation pass: this close-out payload + crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md update (new v2-section paragraph recording today's findings).

summary: PI-002 (DEC-043's option C) shipped. The v2 storage REST API now accepts an omitted or null `identifier` on `POST` for every prefixed-identifier entity type — decision, session, risk, planning_item, and topic join the twelve already-conforming methodology and governance entity types from v0.4+. The access-layer mechanism is the same SAVEPOINT-retry helper proven by the domain repository under concurrency. Backward compatibility is automatic: every existing caller that supplies an explicit identifier continues to work, subject to the format-regex tightening that surfaces a small set of legacy test-fixture prefix corrections (silently accepted before, now properly enforced). CLAUDE.md retires the post-SES-010 'compute client-side' rule. The asymmetry that SES-010 named is resolved; the `GET /<entity>/next-identifier` helpers (DEC-043 option B) remain available for clients that prefer read-then-write.
title: PI-002 build closure — server-side identifier auto-assignment retrofit for decision/session/risk/planning_item/topic; uniform format enforcement on the explicit-identifier path; option-C of SES-010 shipped
topics_covered: PI-002 plan-then-execute. Plan phase confirmed PI-002's scope is narrower than originally framed: of the twelve prefixed-identifier entity types DEC-043 enumerated, seven (the v0.7 governance entities) already implemented option C, and the v0.4+ methodology entities (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate, engagement) implemented it from the start. The retrofit therefore targets exactly five remaining repositories — decision, session, risk, planning_item, topic — mirroring the `access/repositories/domain.py` SAVEPOINT-retry pattern verbatim. Versioned entities (charter, status) are server-assigned by construction; references use numeric REF-NNNN already auto-assigned; both answer PI-002's open design question (2) negatively. Open design question (1) on omitted-vs-empty-string identifier was answered by mirroring domain.py's regex-format validation: `None` triggers auto-assign, `""` fails the prefix regex and returns 422 — same semantics already shipped for v0.4+ entities. Open design question (3) on MCP server impact: no change required; tools call the underlying API and inherit the optional-identifier contract. Execute phase: 5 repositories retrofitted (`_IDENTIFIER_RE`, `_require_identifier_format`, `_increment_identifier`, `_insert_with_autoassign`, `create()` accepts `identifier: str | None = None`); 5 Pydantic Create schemas flipped (`identifier: str` → `identifier: str | None = None`); 30 new access-layer tests across the 5 entity types (omit-assigns-next, supply-uses-it, invalid-format-rejected, empty-string-rejected, dup-explicit-conflict, autoassign-retry-on-collision); 10 new API smoke tests; one collateral fix in `tests/crmbuilder_v2/access/test_exporter.py` (`DEC-EXPLODE` → `DEC-999`) and legacy fixture corrections in `tests/crmbuilder_v2/access/test_risks.py` (`RISK-001` → `RSK-001`), `tests/crmbuilder_v2/access/test_topics.py` (`TOPIC-NNN` → `TOP-NNN`), and `tests/crmbuilder_v2/api/test_risks_planning_topics.py` (same family). The UI-stub identifier `RISK-001` in `tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_context_menus.py:167` was left alone (UI test populates a stub record dict, never invokes `create()` — format regex doesn't run). CLAUDE.md's v2-section paragraph that previously documented the client-side compute-identifier rule (post-SES-010 historical note) was rewritten to its successor form per PI-002's consequence statement on DEC-043. Test suite: 1549 passed, 3 skipped, 0 failed (was 1548 passed + 1 failed after the initial retrofit, fixed in-loop). Out-of-band: PI-002's description was prepended with an 'Explain it like I was 5' summary via direct API PATCH prior to the build, at Doug's request. Status flip: PI-002 was prematurely flipped to Resolved mid-conversation when Doug noted the DB still showed Open; reverted to Open before this close-out drafted so the apply's resolves edge does the canonical Open → Resolved transition via slice A's atomic edge+flip.

title: PI-052 chat-UI design + slicing — architecture for the PySide6 chat tab on the Anthropic API with native tool definitions; eleven decisions settled across Q1–Q10 + tool surface + auth + model + slicing + methodology
topics_covered: Read-first per kickoff Tier 1–5: v2 CLAUDE.md / get_current_status (v0.6 shipped) / DEC-244 (claude.ai connector bug) / DEC-245 (architectural pivot to chat UI on Anthropic API) / PI-052 / SES-077 close-out; inspection of `crmbuilder_v2.mcp_server.tools` (44 functions, FastMCP `@server.tool()` registration pattern, all wrap `httpx.AsyncClient` against the REST API at 127.0.0.1:8765), `crmbuilder_v2.api.main` (FastAPI surface), `crmbuilder_v2.ui.main_window` (sidebar + QStackedWidget + RefreshService pattern, sidebar groups by entity category), `crmbuilder_v2.ui.app` (QApplication factory with Fusion-style + light palette + bundled Inter/JetBrains Mono per design pass), `crmbuilder_v2.config.Settings` (pydantic-settings, CRMBUILDER_V2_* env prefix). Architecture design: PySide6 chat tab in a new 'AI' sidebar group, ChatPanel owning ChatController owning ChatWorker (QThread hosting private asyncio loop), anthropic.AsyncAnthropic + httpx.AsyncClient inside the worker, tool dispatcher reusing mcp_server.tools surface via a Slice-C refactor that extracts a shared `tool_definitions()` helper feeding both FastMCP registration and the chat dispatcher. Worked through all ten kickoff open questions Q1–Q10 plus the goal-list strategic choices (tool surface scope, auth, model selection, slicing). Eleven decisions settled: DEC-252 (PySide6 tab as production surface — resolves DEC-245 Phase 2 deferred), DEC-253 (all 44 tools registered, partitioned by read/write for mode toggle — no MVP subset), DEC-254 (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env primary + system keyring fallback, never settings.json), DEC-255 (auto-execute tool calls with inline disclosure, per-session 'ask before write' toggle), DEC-256 (persistence to JSON files under ~/.crmbuilder-v2/chats/, governance-entity elevation deferred to candidate PI), DEC-257 (streaming on, QThread + asyncio bridge no qasync dep), DEC-258 (claude.ai-web sidebar pattern, single active chat), DEC-259 (per-turn footer + per-session header for cost/tokens with three-tier cache-hit display), DEC-260 (Opus 4.7 default, Sonnet 4.6 + Haiku 4.5 selectable in header), DEC-261 (slicing plan A–D — A terminal spike ~80 LOC, B PySide6 MVP tab with one tool, C full surface + persistence + caching + system prompt drafted, D polish + exports + error matrix), DEC-262 (hybrid kickoff pattern with WT inline summary + file pointer + 'file is canonical' rule, established as the standard for non-trivial kickoff bodies — settles the methodology question raised at the end of the kickoff). Three open questions deferred: Q4 (tool-result visibility default — follows from DEC-255 + design pattern, no DEC), Q6 (system prompt design — outlined in design doc §13, full settlement at Slice C with its own DEC), Q7 (conversation export as governance close-out — surfaced as PI-053 candidate, not adopted in v1). Design doc covers component breakdown, data flow, prompt caching strategy (three tiers: system block + tools block + rolling conversation prefix), UI layout sketch with header / sidebar / transcript / input, slicing plan with explicit acceptance criteria per slice, error recovery matrix (8 failure modes with defined behaviors including 401 / 429 / context-window-exceeded / Stop / app-close / keyring-unavailable), future PI candidates (PI-053 governance-entity elevation, PI-054 remote chat surface, PI-055 tool surface expansion), and an Appendix A diff sketch for the mcp_server.tools refactor that lets both FastMCP and the chat dispatcher share one source of truth.

conversation_reference: Claude Code orchestrator session at Doug's terminal, 2026-05-25 evening, opened in /home/doug/Dropbox/Projects/crmbuilder. The conversation began with Doug asking Claude Code to act as an orchestrator for agents to execute all work related to PI-003, PI-004, and PI-005, and to ensure all three PIs are properly closed out at the end including DB updates and a JSON file written to git. The orchestrator surface-and-settled the scope before dispatching agents: PI-003/004/005 are explicitly deferred and conditional on CBM-paper-test surfacing real need; together they represent six new methodology entity types plus a major process schema growth; full end-to-end build (migrations + access + REST + MCP + UI + tests) is realistically a multi-day workstream that cannot complete in one session. The orchestrator presented four options (commit specs + per-entity build prompts; full parallel build; minimum-viable-backend-only; persona-pilot-then-decide) and Doug chose the per-entity-build-prompts approach: commit the six design specs as a milestone, then author one CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-build-<entity>.md per entity so each future Claude Code session executes its entity end-to-end with its own close-out atomically resolving the relevant PI. At close-out time the orchestrator discovered ses_079.json (parallel-sandbox WS-012 orchestrator-architecture work; claims SES-079 / CONV-049 / PI-053..062 / DEC-246..251) and ses_080.json (parallel-sandbox PI-052 chat-UI design work; claims SES-080 / CONV-050 / WT-048 / DEC-252..262) staged-but-unapplied in git and re-keyed this session's identifiers to SES-081 / CONV-051 / WT-049..054 per the SES-077 re-keying precedent.
in_flight_at_end: PI-003, PI-004, PI-005 remain `Open` at this session's close. Their resolution paths are wired: this session's `addresses_planning_items` edges advance each PI; six future Claude Code sessions consume the six build prompts (WT-049..054) and atomically resolve their respective PIs via slice A's `resolves` reference behavior. Recommended execution sequence per dependencies: persona (PI-003) and field (PI-004 portion) first — they're independent foundational entities; requirement, manual_config, test_spec next — they depend on field/persona for their cross-references; process-v2 last — it depends on persona and field for `process_performed_by_persona` and `process_touches_field` edges. The four PI-004 siblings may run in any order; the last to ship runs the build-closure detection and resolves PI-004. WS-003 (Methodology entity schema design) is the natural workstream affiliation for this and the six successor sessions — even though WS-003 is `complete` against its v0.4 four-entity scope, the v0.5+ extension work is a coherent continuation. No new planning items surfaced from the design phase; each spec's section 3.8 enumerates spec-deferred questions categorized as [v0.5+ build] / [CBM redo] / [v0.6+] — those become PIs in the respective build sessions' close-outs as needed. Ses_079.json (orchestrator architecture, claims SES-079) and ses_080.json (PI-052 chat-UI, claims SES-080) remain staged-but-unapplied alongside this payload; whichever session applies first claims its identifier and the other two need no re-keying because each was authored against an explicit identifier set already non-conflicting with the others.
summary: Six methodology-entity schema specs and six matching build prompts authored to satisfy PI-003, PI-004, and PI-005 incrementally across six future Claude Code sessions. The design phase landed 2,909 lines of new spec documentation under `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schema-specs/` and an estimated 2,400–3,600 lines of build-prompt documentation under `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/`. The three PIs remain `Open` at this session's close and advance via the `addresses` edges this session creates; resolution flips to `Resolved` happen in the future build sessions' close-outs. The six build prompts are recorded as `kickoff_prompt` work_tickets (WT-049..054) anchored to their target PIs, completing the v2 session-lifecycle pattern documented in CLAUDE.md: every unit of work has a planning_item, an addressing kickoff work_ticket pointing to a canonical kickoff `.md` file, and a future session that consumes the WT and lands the implementation.
title: Methodology-schema design phase for PI-003 / PI-004 / PI-005 — six schema specs + six build prompts
topics_covered: Orchestration scope-setting against three Open methodology-evolution PIs (PI-003 persona, PI-004 the four-entity cohort field/requirement/manual_config/test_spec, PI-005 process schema growth). Identifier-prefix non-collision analysis against the full v2 prefix set (DEC/SES/RSK/PI/TOP/REF/CHR/STA/DOM/ENT/PROC/CRM/WS/CONV/RB/WT/COP/DEP/CM) — assigned PER (persona), FLD (field), REQ (requirement), MCF (manual_config), TST (test_spec); process keeps PROC. Cross-spec relationship-kind registration discipline (once-per-kind on the source side; sibling entity types referenced as anticipated inbound until they land). Six parallel design agents dispatched, each producing a ~450–515-line spec following the methodology-entity-schema-spec-guide template. Six parallel build-prompt agents dispatched, each producing a ~400–600-line CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-build-<entity>.md following the existing pi-029-A build-prompt structural template. Build-closure pattern documented for the PI-004 four-sibling cohort (the LAST sibling to ship is the resolver — uses runtime query of `addresses` edges on PI-004 to detect that the other three siblings have landed before flipping `resolves_planning_items`). Process-v2 growth additive-migration story documented (six new TEXT NULL columns via ALTER TABLE; three new relationship kinds; no status change; no data backfill; v0.4 records survive intact). PI-003 and PI-005 each ship via a single build session that resolves the PI atomically; PI-004 ships via four sibling build sessions, the last of which resolves PI-004 via runtime build-closure detection. Per-spec design decisions captured inline in each spec's section 3.9.1 as DEC-XXX placeholders (NOT authored to the v2 governance DB in this session; each build session's close-out authors its spec's decisions with definitive DEC-NNN numbers).

summary: PI-004 first slice (`field` methodology entity type) shipped end-to-end. The v2 storage layer now hosts field records — one attribute on one CRM-modeled entity, surfaced in evolved-methodology Phase 3 iteration build conversations and feeding the downstream YAML deploy spec. The build mirrors the v0.4 slice-C entity / v0.5+ persona pattern with two bounded field-specific deviations per `field.md` v1.0: (1) atomic POST — `POST /fields` requires `field_belongs_to_entity_identifier` in the body so row + edge land in one transaction preserving the live-edge invariant on the mandatory 1:1 affiliation (DEC-271); (2) per-entity-scoped `field_name` uniqueness — `Contact.status` and `Mentor.status` are both valid because the uniqueness key is `(parent_entity_identifier, lower(field_name))`, not `field_name` alone (DEC-269). Plus an 11-value `field_type` enum vocabulary (text/long_text/enum/multi_enum/date/datetime/money/boolean/number/reference/derived; DEC-271). The references repository gains 1:1-mandatory cardinality enforcement on the `field_belongs_to_entity` kind via two new guards: POST-second-edge rejected with 422 `cardinality_violation`; DELETE-only-edge-of-live-field rejected with 422 `cardinality_violation` (the internal `_skip_cardinality_check=True` flag enables the legitimate `delete_field` repo-to-repo path that atomically soft-deletes the row and the edge together). Master-pane primary grouping by parent entity per §3.6.2 is deferred to a follow-on slice — v1 ships flat with a TODO. 'Move to entity' reparenting affordance per §3.6.5 also deferred. PI-004 is ADDRESSED here (not resolved); the last sibling (requirement / manual_config / test_spec) resolves it per the DEC-232 / SES-074 build-closure pattern.
title: PI-004 first slice — `field` methodology entity type end-to-end (migration, access, REST, UI, tests)
topics_covered: Execution of all 18 build steps for the field methodology entity per the build prompt: (1) Alembic migration 0014 creating the `fields` table (11 columns including the `field_previous_parent_entity_identifier` stash supporting §3.4.6 soft-delete/restore atomicity, 4 CHECK constraints, 3 indexes) and extending `refs.source_type` / `refs.target_type` / `refs.relationship_kind` / `change_log.entity_type` CHECKs in a single recopy; (2) `Field` SQLAlchemy model in `access/models.py` mirroring `Entity` with `field_*` prefix and adding the stash column; (3) vocab.py additions — `FIELD_STATUSES`, `FIELD_STATUS_TRANSITIONS`, `FIELD_TYPES` (11 values), `'field'` added to `ENTITY_TYPES`, `'field_belongs_to_entity'` added to `REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS`, one clause added to `_kinds_for_pair`, docstring updated; (4) `FieldCreateIn` / `FieldReplaceIn` / `FieldPatchIn` Pydantic schemas — CreateIn declares the REQUIRED `field_belongs_to_entity_identifier` body key per §3.5.4, Replace/Patch do NOT accept it; (5) `access/repositories/field.py` repository with the eight functions plus helpers — `_require_live_entity` surfaces the §3.5.4 422 shapes, `_reject_duplicate_name_within_entity` queries refs to scope uniqueness per entity, `create_field` does the atomic row+edge+emit in one transaction, `delete_field` stashes the parent and hard-deletes the edge via the `_skip_cardinality_check=True` flag, `restore_field` reads the stash and recreates the edge atomically; (6) references-repo cardinality guards — `_guard_field_belongs_to_entity_delete` helper plus a create-time block rejecting a second edge from the same source field, both surfaces returning 422 with `cardinality_violation`; (7) `api/routers/field.py` with the eight endpoints including the `?entity_identifier=` filter; (8) main.py router registration; (9) eight client methods in ui/client.py with the `entity_identifier` and `include_deleted` query params; (10) sidebar entry appended to Methodology group at position #6 (re-keyed from spec's planned #5 — persona shipped first); (11) main_window dispatch + ENTITY_TYPE_TO_SIDEBAR_LABEL entry; (12) `FieldsPanel` panel mirroring `PersonasPanel` with field-specific adjustments — parent-entity rendered at detail-pane position #3 outside the generic ReferencesSection (mandatory 1:1 affiliation is conceptually part of the field's identity per §3.6.3), required field as read-only disabled checkbox, master-pane entity-grouping deferred to follow-on slice with TODO comment per §3.6.2; (13) `FieldCreateDialog` / `FieldEditDialog` / `FieldDeleteDialog` + `_field_schema.py` — CreateDialog includes a parent-entity combo picker populated from live entities, EditDialog excludes it (reparenting per §3.6.5 deferred to follow-on slice), `field_required` modelled as string combo over ("false","true") with dialog-side coercion to Python bool because the EntityCrudDialog base supports only string-valued widgets;
